`TIER T5 COMPLETE: 2026-04-30. Prompts: 21. A: 9. P: 0. S: 0. N: 12. New findings: 0. SD pointers: 0.`

---
session_b_findings.finding: T2.8.1 data was silent on constitutional body deposit. T5.7 is formally closed.

From v1 Unit 5 correlation signals: R073 guilt (51 shared verses — dominant by far; structural partner), R111 mercy (15 shared), R135 repentance (12), R059 faith (9), R187 strength (9), R197 authority (9), R103 love (8), R184 spirit (7), R043 desire (6), R147 sin (5). Shared anchors: Jer 31:34 (with guilt, love, thought), Mat 5:24 (reconciliation), Mar 3:29 (sin), Lev 4:20 (mercy). OBS-064-011, OBS-064-012.

The co-occurrence pattern reveals forgiveness as embedded in a dense structural network. The guilt-073 signal (51 shared verses) confirms guilt as the definitional counterpart — forgiveness addresses guilt; they are structurally inseparable. The mercy-111 and repentance-135 signals confirm the divine-side and human-side structural partners respectively. The love-103 signal (8 shared verses) confirms the downstream love-response as a recurring correlation. OBS-064-011.

Forgiveness consistently follows: guilt (the prior condition forgiveness addresses), repentance (the access-posture), and mercy (the divine disposition that generates the act). It consistently precedes: love (Luk 7:47 — love is the downstream product), direct knowledge of God (Jer 31:34), and the capacity to extend forgiveness to others. OBS-064-011, OBS-064-017, OBS-064-029.

Causal in key sequences: guilt → forgiveness is not merely sequential but addresses-and-resolves (forgiveness is the specific answer to guilt's condition). Forgiveness → love (Luk 7:47) is explicitly causal and quantitatively proportional. The sequence is not merely developmental but causally structured: guilt causally occasions forgiveness; forgiveness causally produces love. OBS-064-029.

Forgiveness produces: love (Luk 7:47), reverential fear (Psa 130:4), direct knowledge of God (Jer 31:34), capacity to worship (Mat 5:24), capacity to extend forgiveness (Mat 18:35 structural logic). These are the named downstream characteristics. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-020, OBS-064-021, OBS-064-028, OBS-064-029.

Forgiveness is produced by: mercy (R111 — the divine disposition that generates the forgiving act), divine initiative (God's character and promise), and the atoning mechanism (Christ's blood — Eph 1:7). OBS-064-019, OBS-064-028.

Forgiveness is constituent of: reconciliation (R130 — Mat 5:24 shared anchor; reconciliation presupposes forgiveness as its inner ground); the new covenant (Jer 31:34 — forgiveness is one of the defining elements of the new covenant); love-formation (Luk 7:47 — forgiveness is constitutive of the love-response). OBS-064-017, OBS-064-028, OBS-064-029.

H5545 salach shares no vocabulary with other terms (no XREF terms — Unit 2 confirmed zero). G0863 aphiēmi shares vocabulary with G0863G/H/I (programme sub-entries). The guilt-073 co-occurrence at 51 shared verses is the strongest vocabulary-field overlap — though not at the root-sharing level. OBS-064-003.

The salach root is lexically self-contained — no root-sharing with other programme registries. The aphiēmi root shares structural logic with the releasing/departure vocabulary in G0863G (leaving-fellowship vocabulary). Root-level isolation of H5545 salach is analytically significant — it maintains a lexically distinct divine-exclusive domain. OBS-064-003.

The salach root's lexical isolation at the divine-exclusive level reveals that forgiveness has a divine-only core vocabulary that is structurally separate from the human forgiveness vocabulary. The act-structure is shared (releasing a claim — both Hebrew and Greek), but the lexical-root domain is asymmetric: only God forgives with salach. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-006.

R073 guilt is the closest adjacent characteristic — it is the prior condition forgiveness addresses. The distinction: guilt names the condition (the state of being under moral debt); forgiveness names the act that removes it. They are structurally adjacent as condition and remedy. R135 repentance is the nearest human-side access-posture — the inner turn that positions the person to receive forgiveness. OBS-064-011, OBS-064-013.

Forgiveness vs guilt: kind distinction — guilt is the condition; forgiveness is the remedy. Forgiveness vs mercy (R111): layer distinction — mercy is the disposition that generates forgiving; forgiveness is the act that mercy produces. Forgiveness vs reconciliation (R130): sequence distinction — forgiveness may precede reconciliation as its inner ground; reconciliation is the restored relational state that forgiveness makes possible. OBS-064-011, OBS-064-028.

Forgiveness vs guilt: kind (condition vs remedy). Forgiveness vs mercy: layer (disposition vs act). Forgiveness vs repentance: sequential (access-posture precedes receipt). Forgiveness vs reconciliation: sequential (inner ground precedes relational state). OBS-064-011.
Jer 31:34 — shared with R073 guilt, R103 love, R160 thought. Mat 5:24 — shared with R130 reconciliation. Mar 3:29 — shared with R147 sin. Lev 4:20 — shared with R111 mercy. OBS-064-012.

Jer 31:34's triple sharing (guilt, love, thought) reveals it as the highest-convergence verse in the forgiveness corpus — the new covenant promise joins forgiveness, guilt-resolution, love-formation, and cognitive-relational access (they shall know me) in a single text. This convergence is analytically significant for Session D synthesis. OBS-064-012.

Primary dimension (Relational Disposition — 10/14 groups) shared with love, mercy, fellowship, and other relational-disposition registries. Secondary dimensions: Volition (shared with agency/power registries), Moral Character (shared with virtue registries), Dependence/Creatureliness (shared with trust/prayer registries), Divine-Human Correspondence (unique to the unforgivable sin outer limit). OBS-064-007.

The Relational Disposition primary dimension shared with mercy (R111) and love (R103) positions forgiveness within the relational triad operating in C17: mercy is the disposition that generates forgiving; forgiveness is the act; love is the response. The dimensional sharing reveals structural adjacency within the cluster. OBS-064-007.

H5545 (sa.lach — verb): to forgive; exclusive divine subject. H5546 (sal.lach — adjective): forgiving as divine character; Psa 86:5 sole occurrence. H5547 (se.li.chah — noun): forgiveness as divine possession; Psa 130:4, Dan 9:9, Neh 9:17. G0859 (afesis — noun): forgiveness as state/condition; predominantly in "forgiveness of sins" phrase. G0863G/H/I: three sub-entries of aphiēmi ("to release: leave"; "to release: forgive"; "to release: permit"). Root meaning H5545: uncertain etymology — a technical term for divine forgiveness with no clear concrete root image. Root meaning G0863: apo (away from) + hiēmi (to send/let go) = to send away, release a hold. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-004, OBS-064-005.

H5545 grammatical range: Qal (active — God forgives), Niphal (passive — "they shall be forgiven" — the worshipper as receiver in the cultic context). The Niphal passive is the covenantal-objective mode's grammatical form — the worshipper receives the declared status without being named as the active agent. G0863 (aphiēmi) range: active (forgive, permit, leave), passive (be forgiven). G0859 (afesis) is a state-noun — the condition produced by the act. The grammatical range distinguishes: divine act (Qal H5545, active G0863H) vs creaturely received condition (Niphal H5545, G0859 noun). OBS-064-003, OBS-064-005.

Semantic range: (1) Divine cultic declaration (H5545 Niphal — "they shall be forgiven" in the atonement formula); (2) Divine character attribute (H5546 — God's dispositional readiness); (3) Divine possession (H5547 — forgiveness as quality God has); (4) State of being forgiven (G0859); (5) Personal declaratory forgiveness (G0863H — "your sins are forgiven"); (6) Commanded horizontal forgiveness (G0863H — human commanded to release claim); (7) Releasing attachment (G0863G — leaving family, goods for discipleship); (8) Permitting/granting access (G0863I); (9) Withholding permission (G0863I — moral obstruction). OBS-064-003, OBS-064-009, OBS-064-010.

The vocabulary distinguishes: disposition (H5546 — God's character-readiness to forgive) vs act (H5545 — God's performing the act) vs possession (H5547 — the quality God has). Act vs state: G0863H (forgiving act) vs G0859 (state of having been forgiven). Given (God's act — Qal H5545) vs received (human's condition — Niphal H5545, G0859). The grammar is precise about who holds and who receives the initiative. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-005, OBS-064-019.

The unforgivable sin (Mar 3:29 — G0863H negative form with "never," "eternal condemnation") is the explicit outer-limit negative. The structural negative of forgiveness is unforgiveness in the withholder (Mat 18:34-35 — the tormentors-state). Both are present: the lexical outer-limit negative (Mar 3:29) and the inner-being consequence of withheld forgiveness (Mat 18:34). OBS-064-027, OBS-064-032.

H5546 (sal.lach — adjective) names the person-type: "you, O Lord, are good and forgiving (sal.lach)" — God as the dispositional forgiver. G2844-adjacent: no specific "forgiver" person-type term but the agent is named by the active verb construction. The state-noun G0859 (afesis) identifies the one in the state of forgiveness. The person-type vocabulary is asymmetric: the Forgiver (divine, uniquely designated by H5546) and the forgiven (the one in the G0859 state). OBS-064-019.

Yes — multiple supplication/seeking terms. Dan 9:19 ("O Lord, forgive" — direct supplication using H5545 imperative). Act 2:38 (Peter's command to "be baptized for the forgiveness of your sins" — which responds to the crowd's "what shall we do?" as supplication). 1Jo 1:9 (confession as the seeking-act). The seeking of forgiveness is a rich vocabulary event — petition, confession, intercession. OBS-064-015, OBS-064-022.

The LXX applied aphiēmi/afesis to render OT forgiveness vocabulary (H5545 → aphiēmi in the cultic passages), and the NT continues this rendering — extending it to commanded human practice. The cross-testament development is the use of the same Greek vocabulary for both divine and human forgiveness, which is not present in the Hebrew (where H5545 is exclusively divine). This lexical bridging is analytically significant: the NT does not create a new vocabulary for human forgiveness but applies the divine-forgiveness vocabulary to the commanded human act. OBS-064-006.

The vocabulary arc reveals forgiveness as a characteristic with two distinct lexical domains — a divine-exclusive Hebrew core (H5545 — salach) and a structurally shared Greek vocabulary (aphiēmi — used for both divine and human forgiveness). The arc moves from OT restriction (forgiveness is exclusively divine) to NT expansion (the same act is commanded of humans) through lexical bridging. This arc is unique in the programme: forgiveness is the one characteristic where the cross-testament vocabulary development carries an explicit structural claim — that human forgiveness is shaped by and modelled on divine forgiveness. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-006.

Psa 130:4 — H5547 (se.li.chah noun) functions as the ground of hope: "there is forgiveness with you, that you may be feared." The noun functions as the asserted divine quality on which the psalmist's hope rests. The function is declaratory-of-divine-character rather than describing an act already performed or requesting one. OBS-064-020.

Literary forms: (1) Cultic/legal corpus — Lev 4:20, Num 15:25 (atonement formula); (2) Prophetic promise — Jer 31:34 (new covenant), Isa 53:5 (servant); (3) Penitential psalm — Psa 130 (waiting for forgiveness), Dan 9:19 (intercession); (4) Synoptic narrative — Mat 18:23-35, Luk 7:36-50 (forgiveness in encounter and parable); (5) Apostolic letter — Eph 1:7, Col 2:13, Eph 4:32. Each literary form carries a distinct mode: cultic = objective declaration; prophecy = eschatological promise; psalm = seeking posture; narrative = relational encounter; epistle = theological ground and ethical command. OBS-064-014.

Luk 7:47 argument structure: "her many sins have been forgiven — that is why she loved much; but one who is forgiven little loves little." The causal logic: magnitude of forgiveness → proportional love. This is a diagnostic-interpretive argument: Jesus uses the love visible in the woman's act to reveal the magnitude of her forgiveness. The argument structure is: love-visible → forgiveness-inferred; forgiveness-magnitude → love-magnitude (bidirectional inference). OBS-064-029.

Settings: Cultic-legal (authoritative declaration within covenant ritual), Prophetic-eschatological (new covenant promise), Penitential (seeking posture — deepest human approach to God), Parabolic-narrative (encounter and conflict — Synoptic forgiveness controversies), Apostolic-theological (grounding and commanding). The parabolic-narrative setting carries the most developed inner-being analysis of forgiveness (Mat 18 — the withholder's inner torment; Luk 7 — the love-response). OBS-064-014.

Jer 31:34 is the architecturally complete verse — it names the divine action (I will forgive), the comprehensive scope (their iniquity), and the permanent cognitive-relational consequence (remember their sin no more). Psa 86:5 is the most complete divine character statement (good and forgiving and abounding in steadfast love). Luk 7:47 is the most developed inner-being consequence statement. No single verse holds all three — the primary anchor trio is Jer 31:34, Psa 86:5, and Luk 7:47. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-019, OBS-064-029.

Jer 31:34 uniquely reveals forgiveness as the ground of permanent direct knowledge of God — the guilt-barrier's removal is the prerequisite for unmediated divine-human cognition. No other verse makes this cognitive-relational consequence this explicit. Luk 7:47 uniquely reveals the love-production as quantitatively proportional to the forgiveness received — a causal mechanism visible nowhere else in the corpus. Psa 86:5 uniquely attests the adjectival person-type designation for the divine forgiver — the sole occurrence of sal.lach in the OT. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-019, OBS-064-029.

Two frameworks: (1) Restorative justice theory — the cancellation of debt model maps onto restorative justice's vocabulary (restoration of right relation without full penalty extraction). (2) Moral psychology of forgiveness — the distinction between forgiveness as inner release vs reconciliation as restored relationship maps onto clinical psychology's forgiveness theory. Framework identification only. OBS-064-001, OBS-064-032.

Moral psychology of forgiveness illuminates the inner-being cost of unforgiveness — the "torment" of Mat 18:34 is consistent with clinical evidence that unforgiveness produces inner-being damage (rumination, resentment, physiological stress). The biblical account provides the interpretive frame (inner imprisonment, tormentors) that moral psychology fills with clinical description. OBS-064-032.

The biblical account diverges from psychological forgiveness theory in two areas: (1) the vertical-horizontal interdependence (Mat 6:14-15; Mat 18:35) — the idea that one's receipt of forgiveness is affected by one's extension of forgiveness is not a feature of psychological models; (2) the outer limit of the unforgivable sin (Mar 3:29) has no psychological parallel. The divergence reveals that biblical forgiveness is embedded in a relational ontology (vertical-horizontal correspondence) that purely psychological accounts cannot accommodate. OBS-064-027, OBS-064-032.

`TIER T7 COMPLETE: 2026-04-30. Prompts: 20. A: 9. P: 6. S: 1 (T7.1.9). N: 4. New findings: 1 (Q&A-179 — forgiveness is the only characteristic in the programme where the cross-testament vocabulary development carries an explicit structural claim: the NT applies the divine-forgiveness vocabulary to commanded human forgiveness, asserting human forgiveness as shaped by and modelled on divine forgiveness). SD pointers: 0.`

---
session_b_findings.finding: Restorative justice frameworks raise the question of the community's role in forgiveness — whether forgiveness is a purely bilateral (offender-offended) act or involves the broader community. The intercessory mode (Dan 9:19 — Daniel praying for the community) and the corporate cultic atonement (Num 15:25-26 — corporate forgiveness) suggest a communal dimension not captured in individual psychological accounts. Gap noted for Session D. OBS-064-015, OBS-064-016.

Rom 8:32 (anchor 888-001) reveals God as one who does not spare his own Son but gives — giving at supreme personal cost as the defining expression of divine character. Eph 2:8 establishes grace as sovereign, unmerited divine disposition. Exo 33:17 (anchor 889-001) reveals grace grounded in prior divine knowing and personal address. The divine character reflected: one who gives freely to the undeserving, whose giving is not exhausted by cost, and whose favour is rooted in personal knowledge of the recipient.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: the prompt clarifies that what is reflected is not merely generosity but the combination of sovereign freedom, personal knowing, and uncaused bestowal.*

Yes — extensively. G5485 charis in group 888-001 names grace as "sovereign, unmerited divine disposition." H2580 chen in 889-001 names "divine relational favour." G5487 charitoō (Luk 1:28 anchor) names God's act of bestowing grace as a completed act that transforms the recipient's standing. The attribution reveals that when grace appears in the human person — as character quality (889-003), as forgiving act (5470-002), as communicative quality (SD022) — it is always derivative: the human expression of grace is a secondary reflection of the divine original.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: the derivative character of human grace is made explicit as a T0-level claim.*

Eph 2:8: grace as the means through which the person is constituted as saved. 1Cor 15:10: grace as the condition through which the person becomes what they are — identity-constituting. Rom 5:2: grace as the standing in which the person lives. Act 20:32: grace as the constructive force through which the inner person is built up. Together: a coherent four-part functional picture — access (standing before God), identity (becoming someone), formation (being built up), extension (giving what has been received to others).

*Adds structure: prior analysis addressed these functions separately across SD pointers. The prompt consolidates them under a single created-purpose heading.*

The tension is present but not resolved. Grace as unmerited giving presupposes a recipient who has no claim — consistent with both creaturely status before God (the creature always receives; it cannot give to God from its own resources) and fallen status. SD008 (Eph 2:1-5: grace addressing the dead) and SD032 (grace inhabiting weakness) both point toward the fallen condition as the primary context. But whether grace as the relational disposition of the greater toward the lesser belongs to the original created order is not directly resolved.

*Gap identified: the question of grace in the original created order — before the fall, by virtue of being a creature — is not addressed in the prior analysis. This is a genuine T0 gap.*

Yes. SD027 (Eph 2:7): "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us." The present experience of grace is real but partial; its fullness is eschatological. SD031 (Rom 5:2) and SD005 (hope and grace co-constituting the same inner standing) confirm the trajectory.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD027, SD005, SD031). Adds structure: scattered eschatological observations consolidated under a single T0 category.*

When the human person exercises grace — in forgiving (5470-002: charizō carrying both giving and forgiving), in speech quality (charis as gracious words, Luk 4:22, Col 4:6), in character disposition (889-003: gracious disposition expressed in conduct) — they express the likeness of the one whose primary characteristic is to give freely to the undeserving. The image-bearing dimension is the capacity for uncaused giving: giving not triggered by the recipient's merit but by the giver's disposition.

*Adds structure: prior analysis addressed grace-as-human-act primarily in relational terms. The prompt frames it as an image-bearing capacity — a distinct T0-level claim.*

The data is explicit. charizō (G5483) carries both the divine act of giving (God graciously giving all things, Rom 8:32) and the human act of forgiving (Eph 4:32). The same term names both. This is not analogy — it is lexical identity. SD018 raises this precisely: when God forgives, is God doing the same inner-being thing as when God gives? The data does not resolve the question but confirms the lexical identity.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD018). Adds precision: lexical identity is a stronger image-bearing claim than mere resemblance.*

SD043 names acharistos (graceless, ungrateful) as the structural antithesis — the inner failure to register grace as grace. SD004 establishes pride as the counter-condition. SD033 (Eph 2:8) suggests surrender of self-contribution is the necessary inner condition for grace-reception. Together: where grace is absent, the image is impaired — the capacity for uncaused giving suppressed by pride, self-sufficiency, or ingratitude.

*Gap identified: the existing analysis addresses counter-conditions but does not frame them explicitly as image-impairment. The prompt surfaces this as a T0 observation not previously named.*

Yes. SD023: charitoō is a biblical coinage — created for Luk 1:28 (Mary as the one graced), pointing toward the one who is "full of grace and truth" (Joh 1:14, SD042). SD038 reads Zec 12:10 as prophetic-typological: the outpouring of the spirit of grace is eschatological and christological, occasioned by looking on "him whom they have pierced."

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD023, SD038, SD042). Adds structure: these are named as typological deployments.*

Divine-to-human. The divine instance establishes the pattern; the human instance is patterned on it. Eph 4:32: "forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you." SD026 (Zec 12:10: even the human response to grace is grace-given) establishes the most radical form of this directionality.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds category: directional patterns previously noted without naming them as a typological question.*

Grace. The name signals immediately: not merit, not debt, not obligation. The English "grace" carries both the relational sense (favour, goodwill) and the aesthetic sense (gracefulness, beauty) — both present in the Hebrew and Greek vocabulary (chen as both favour and beauty/charm, Prov 31:30; charis as both grace and gracious quality in speech). The name orients the enquiry before analysis begins.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds observation: the name itself encodes the uncaused character — a T1-level naming observation not previously drawn out.*

H2580 chen (favour) signals relational standing — being seen with favour by one who has power to grant or withhold. H8469 tachanum (supplication) signals the receiving side — the posture of the creature appealing for what it cannot command. G5485 charis signals the full semantic arc. Together the primary terms reveal a two-faced definitional structure: the disposition of the giver (chen, charis) and the posture of the receiver (tachanum). The vocabulary embeds the relational architecture before analysis begins: grace requires both a giver and a receiver oriented toward each other.

*Adds structure: the two-faced definitional structure (giver/receiver) as a T1-level observation — the vocabulary encodes the relational architecture at first encounter.*

Yes — directional and relational. The name orients the enquiry toward: a direction of movement (from the greater to the lesser — SD024 encodes this in the CHEN root); a relational structure (giver and receiver — grace is not solitary); a constitutional implication (the receiver is constitutionally dependent). The name does not foreground constitutional location, faculty engagement, or developmental arc — it foregrounds the relational event.

*Adds structure: prior analysis noted these implications separately. The prompt consolidates them as an orienting observation.*

All four kinds — and this is analytically significant. SD021's four semantic faces of charis establish that grace is: a divine **disposition** (face 1: 888-001); a **condition** created in the recipient (face 2: 888-002 — "this grace in which we stand"); a **capacity** gifted into the person (face 3: 888-004); a **quality** expressed outward in character and speech (face 4: 889-003). Grace is not reducible to any single kind. It is a disposition that creates a condition that produces a capacity that expresses itself as a quality — the four faces are sequential and constitutive, not parallel.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD021). Adds structure: the prompt frames the four-face finding as a Kind question — a T1 observation.*

Internally complex at first encounter. The CHEN root family already reveals constituent structure: chen (disposition), chanan (the act of being gracious), chinnam (the logic of uncaused giving), tachanum/techinah (the receiving posture). The Greek similarly: charis, charizō, charitoō each name a distinct operation. The registry's 11 verse context groups confirm — each addressing a distinct operational face.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: complexity is confirmed at first encounter from vocabulary alone.*

The registry header provides: "Grace is undeserved gift — the giving of what is needed not because it has been earned but because the giver chooses to give it... Grace creates the recipient rather than rewarding them." Strong on relational and dispositional character but does not yet encapsulate constitutional location or faculty engagement. The sb_classification gives "Spirit-soul interface" as working constitutional placement.

*Gap identified: the Kind description is strong but incomplete — T2/T3 findings are required before the full working description can be written. Expected at the current stage.*

Multiple structural opposites are named in the data. The primary structural opposite is the logic of merit. SD043 names acharistos (G0884 — graceless, ungrateful) as the lexically embedded opposite. SD004 establishes pride (Reg 123) as the inner-being opposite. SD033 (Eph 2:8) establishes merit-claim as the operative counter-condition. Three distinct faces: acharistos (failure to register grace as gift), pride (elevation of self making grace unnecessary), merit-logic (the framework within which grace cannot operate).

*Adds structure: prior analysis addressed these in different SD pointers. The prompt consolidates them as Boundary — the structural opposite has three faces, each excluding grace differently.*

Grace explicitly excludes: debt (chinnam — for nothing, without cause); merit (Eph 2:8: "not your own doing; it is the gift of God"); self-sufficiency (2Cor 12:9: grace operates in weakness, not strength); ingratitude (acharistos — receiving the gift without registering it as gift); earning as a framework (wages, not grace — the NT merit/gift distinction).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds organisation.*

The data identifies important but unresolved boundaries. Grace is not the same as chesed (SD025 proposes: chen initiates, chesed sustains). Grace is not the same as love (SD013: the boundary question is raised but not resolved). Grace is not mercy (proximity noted, not formally distinguished). Grace is not justice (SD047: open question). These boundary questions require Session D to resolve — the individual registry can identify the boundaries but cannot draw them precisely.

*Gap identified: the precise boundary between grace and its nearest neighbours is raised but not resolved. Boundary is one of the T1 elements that will be rewritten when Session D analysis is complete.*

At least six distinct modes: (1) Sovereign bestowal — grace given from above, constituting the recipient's standing (888-001, 889-001); (2) Relational standing — the condition of being-in-grace (888-002: "this grace in which we stand"); (3) Enabling power — animating force of ministry and identity (888-004; 1Cor 15:10); (4) Forgiving act — release of debt patterned on divine forgiveness (5470-002); (5) Character quality — gracious disposition expressed in speech and conduct (889-003); (6) Supplication posture — the appeal of the creature to the greater for what it cannot supply (890-001).

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD021, 11 verse context groups). Adds structure: six named modes — more complete than SD021's four-face model. Modes 4 (forgiving), 5 (character quality), and 6 (supplication) were not explicitly included in SD021's sequential model.*

Yes — direction is determinative. Grace operates differently depending on whether direction is God-to-human (constituting, bestowing, enabling), human-to-God (appealing, supplicating), or human-to-human (forgiving, expressing, extending). The sb_classification "Spirit-soul interface" suggests operation across constitutional levels — confirmed in SD021's sequential model and SD042 (incarnation as supreme somatic expression).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: direction as the primary mode-determining variable is now explicit at T1 level.*

Yes — explicitly. charis as gracious speech quality appears in Luk 4:22 ("gracious words") and Col 4:6 ("speech seasoned with grace"). SD022 identifies this as the chen/grace-as-speech-quality sense carried into Greek. SD036 adds Act 20:32: "the word of his grace, which is able to build you up" — grace operating constructively through proclaimed speech. The communicative mode is integral, not peripheral.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD022, SD036). Adds structure: the communicative mode confirmed at T1 level as a distinct and integral mode.*

The data surfaces multiple immediate responses — and they are counterintuitive. SD031 (Luk 1:28-30): the first response to grace-announcement is **fear**, not joy. SD038 (Zec 12:10): at the eschatological outpouring the first response is **mourning** — grief at the cost of what has been given. SD039 (Ruth 2:10): the first response to unexpected grace is **prostration** — the body enacting humility before articulation is possible. Fear, mourning, and prostration are the three documented immediate responses in the anchor evidence — all preceding joy, peace, or thanksgiving.

*New finding as a consolidated observation: prior analysis noted these responses in individual SD pointers but did not draw them together as a T1 pattern. The pattern is analytically significant: the immediate inner response to grace is consistently creaturely recognition (fear, grief, prostration) before positive states settle.*

Variation with a common underlying structure. The responses differ (fear, mourning, prostration) but share a common structure: the creature encountering grace is first made aware of the distance between itself and the giver — the weight of what is being received — before moving to reception. Whether this holds across all 194 active verses cannot be confirmed from current data.

*Gap identified: the immediate response pattern is evidenced in three anchor contexts but not systematically examined across the full verse evidence.*

The data is rich. Sustained reception of grace produces: **Identity constitution** (1Cor 15:10: "by the grace of God I am what I am" — SD004); **Inner standing** (Rom 5:2: being-in-grace as relational position — 888-002); **Enabling capacity** (Act 20:32: person built up; 1Cor 15:10: ability to work harder); **Tenderheartedness** (Eph 4:32: eusplanchnos as mediating inner state — SD030); **New appetite/desire reorientation** (SD049: grace possibly reordering desire); **Assurance** (SD029, Rom 8:32: inner confidence from grace-inference — reasoned, not merely felt).

*Consistent with prior analysis (multiple SD pointers). Adds structure: six distinct sustained effects consolidated — prior analysis addressed these individually; the prompt reveals their collective scope.*

States: standing before God (Rom 5:2), identity constituted by grace (1Cor 15:10). Qualities: tenderheartedness (Eph 4:32), gracious speech and conduct (889-003), humility (yielding posture of Eph 2:8). Capacities: ability to work (1Cor 15:10), ability to forgive (5470-002), ability to extend grace to others. Orientations: forward-orientation toward eschatological fullness (Eph 2:7, SD027), dependence-orientation of the creature before God.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds organisation.*

Yes — significantly. The immediate responses (fear, mourning, prostration) are creaturely recognition responses — the first impact of encountering grace. The sustained effects are constitutive — they rebuild and orient the inner person over time. The movement is from recognition of the weight of grace (immediate) to inhabitation of grace as one's standing (sustained). This is not merely temporal but qualitative: the immediate response acknowledges what grace is; the sustained effect is what grace does to the person over time.

*New finding as a consolidated observation: prior analysis noted the sequential character of grace's operation but did not draw the immediate/sustained contrast as a T1 observation. Analytically useful and not previously named in these terms.*

The data identifies: **seeking** (Reg 140, 40-verse cooccurrence — SD002: the inner posture of the creature oriented toward what is outside itself); **receptivity of hearing** (Reg 213, 11 verses — SD012); **yielding** (Reg 180, 8 verses — SD033: surrender of the merit-claim); **humility** (prostration pattern — SD039); **need/vulnerability** (2Cor 12:9: weakness as the condition in which grace operates most fully — SD032). Most significantly: SD026 establishes that the capacity for supplication itself is grace-given — the inner conditions for reception are themselves grace-enabled, not independently generated.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: SD026 creates a theologically important circularity — the conditions of reception are grace-produced — which the prompt makes explicit at T1 level.*

**Pride** (SD004: self-elevating orientation); **merit-logic** (excluded by chinnam and Eph 2:8); **ingratitude** (SD043: acharistos — receiving without registering as gift); **hardened heart refusing to hear** (SD012: the condition that closes off grace); **absence of mourning/recognition** (SD038: grace at its deepest requires the inner recognition of what the pierced one has cost).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds organisation.*

Partially addressed. acharistos (SD043) names the graceless person — one who cannot register gift as gift. SD004 implies the proud person cannot receive what operates in weakness. But the full inner-being state of the non-receiver is not directly described in systematic terms — addressed obliquely through counter-conditions rather than directly.

*Gap identified: the inner state of the person who encounters grace but does not receive it is not directly described. Would require examination of verses where grace is present but refused.*

Relational Disposition — confirmed in the registry header (dimensions = "Relational/Social"), dim_review_status = "Complete." The dominant_subject for most groups is GOD.

*Consistent with prior analysis.*

The relational structure is constitutive of grace at every level: vocabulary requires a giver and receiver; verse groups are organised around directional relationships; anchor verses establish relational events (Exo 33:17: found favour in God's sight; Ruth 2:10: found favour in Boaz's eyes; Eph 4:32: forgiving one another). Grace is unintelligible without the relational structure — it cannot be abstracted from the giving-receiving dynamic. Relational Disposition is therefore not one feature but its constitutive mode.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds precision: relational structure is not a feature but a constitutive mode.*

Yes. Three dimensions across groups: Relational Disposition (primary, most groups), Moral Character (one group — 889-003: gracious character quality), Dependence/Creatureliness (one group — 890-001: supplication as creature's appeal). The three dimensions together map the full relational circuit: God's disposition (Relational Disposition), expressed toward the creature's dependence (Dependence/Creatureliness), producing character in the person (Moral Character). This three-dimensional circuit is not yet formally stated as such in the existing data.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds observation: the circuit-framing is new.*

Yes. Zec 12:10: "I will pour out on the house of David... a spirit of grace and pleas for mercy." The term "spirit of grace" locates the outpouring at the spirit level. The sb_classification confirms: "Spirit-soul interface." The spirit level is the primary reception point of grace — received at the deepest constitutional level.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds precision: spirit level is the point of reception, not of generation — grace arrives there from God.*

The origin question is directional: grace originates with God and is received by the human spirit. It does not originate in the human spirit — it arrives there. Spirit-level location in the human constitution is the point of reception, not the point of generation. This distinction is important and leads directly to T2.9 (Origin and Source).

*Adds precision: prior analysis used "spirit level" without consistently distinguishing location from origin. A useful T2 clarification.*

Grace reaches the deepest level of the person — the primary site of encounter with God. It does not operate only at the level of feeling, thought, or behaviour. SD008 (Eph 2:1-5: grace addressing the dead — the person without inner resource at the deepest level) and SD026 (grace producing even the capacity for supplication — reaching to the level of the person's capacity for response) both confirm that spirit-level operation means grace can reach what nothing else can reach.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T2 framing.*

Yes. Rom 5:2 ("this grace in which we stand") and 1Cor 15:10 ("by the grace of God I am what I am") both describe grace as a constitutive condition of the inner personal experience — the soul level. The standing, the identity, the capacity for effort — all soul-level expressions of what was received at the spirit level.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T2 framing: the soul level is where grace's effects become part of the person's stable inner condition.*

That grace is not merely a transient encounter but a constitutive inner condition. SD021's sequential model makes this explicit: divine disposition received at spirit level → relational standing created at soul level → specific capacity in the person → character expressed outward.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD021).*

The heart is not explicitly named as the location of grace in primary anchor verses. However, Eph 4:32 names tenderheartedness (eusplanchnos — gut/heart compassion) as the mediating inner state that grace produces. This is heart-adjacent: grace produces a condition of the heart rather than being explicitly located there itself.

*Gap identified: heart-location of grace is not explicitly established. eusplanchnos is as close as the data comes — and this is a mediating effect, not a primary location.*

Where eusplanchnos is the evidence (Eph 4:32, SD030), it reveals that grace's operation produces compassion-at-the-gut-level — affective at the deepest somatic-inner interface. This confirms grace reaches the affective level of the inner person, not only the cognitive or relational. A T2 and T3 (affect) finding simultaneously.

The mind is implicitly engaged. SD029 (Rom 8:32): grace producing inner confidence through reasoning — the person thinks their way into assurance that grace makes available. SD048 raises the question of whether right thinking about grace is itself a grace-enabled inner act. But grace is not explicitly located in the mind — the cognitive engagement is a consequence or mode of operation, not a constitutional location.

*Gap identified: mind-location is not established. Cognitive engagement with grace is real but frames it as a mode of operation (T1.4) rather than a constitutional location.*

Where grace engages cognition (Rom 8:32 inference-structure), it reveals that grace produces a particular form of inner knowing — reasoned confidence derived from the logic of what God has done. This cognitive form of assurance operates independently of experiential feeling.

*Adds precision: grace operating cognitively independently of affect is a finding worth retaining even though mind is not established as a constitutional location.*

Two candidates surface. The will (SD010: 11 verses with will, Reg 173 — the question of how grace and volition relate) and the affective subset (eusplanchnos — gut-compassion). Neither is formally established as a soul subset for grace in the current data.

*Gap identified: candidates present but not formally established.*

Yes — extensively. **The eye** is the systematic somatic encoding of grace in the Hebrew chen corpus: SD041 identifies approximately 20 of 67 chen verses using "found favour in the eyes of..." — grace as being-beheld by the favourable gaze. **The supplication body**: prostration, weeping, fasting, sackcloth and ashes (Dan 9:3, SD001, SD038, SD039). **Gut/inner parts** (eusplanchnos — Eph 4:32, SD030). **The body as a whole** in Joh 1:14 (the Word became flesh — SD042).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds organisation: three distinct body-links — the eye (constituting standing), the supplication body (enacting inner posture), eusplanchnos (mediating grace outward).*

- **The eye** (SD041): **constitutive** significance — the divine gaze constitutes the person's standing. Grace is being-seen by the one whose seeing matters.
- **The supplication body** (prostration, weeping, fasting — SD001, SD039): **expressive and indicative** — "the body enacts what the inner person is doing... the whole person brought into alignment with the inner posture of plea."
- **Eusplanchnos** (Eph 4:32, SD030): **functional** — the inner parts function as the mediating organ through which grace received flows outward as grace extended.
- **The flesh of the incarnation** (Joh 1:14, SD042): **paradigmatic** — the supreme somatic expression of grace; grace takes bodily form at its fullest.

*Adds structure: all significance types (constitutive, expressive, indicative, functional, paradigmatic) are present across the grace corpus — an unusually complete somatic coverage.*

Both directions are evidenced. **Soul → body**: the inner posture of supplication and dependence is enacted somatically — prostration, weeping, fasting express the inner orientation outwardly (SD001, SD039). Gracious character quality expresses itself in gracious speech and conduct (889-003, SD022). **Body → soul (relational event → inner condition)**: the eye-formula — when God's eyes rest favourably on a person, this constitutes their standing before him. A relational-somatic event creates an inner condition.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T2 framing: grace operates bidirectionally through the body-soul interface — a constitutional finding.*

Grace is a whole-person phenomenon. The body is integral to its operation — not merely an occasional expression. Grace cannot be adequately described as an inner-only phenomenon. It has a constitutive somatic dimension in both directions.

*New finding as a T2-level statement: prior analysis noted somatic elements but did not draw the bidirectionality conclusion at constitutional level.*

SD042 (Joh 1:14: grace taking permanent bodily form in the incarnation) is the most radical body-deposit statement but is a unique christological event. The tenderheartedness (eusplanchnos, Eph 4:32) produced by grace may constitute a sustained inner-constitutional deposit — a changed affective responsiveness — but whether this constitutes a bodily deposit in the T2.8 sense is not addressed in the data.

*Gap identified: the constitutional deposit question is not resolved for Grace.*

Unambiguously: grace originates with God. It is received from outside the human person — not generated from within. This is constitutive of the definition. chinnam (H2600: for nothing, without cause) names the structural logic: grace is given for nothing, without cause in the recipient. SD026 radicalises this: even the capacity to receive grace (the supplication posture) is grace-given. Grace originates entirely outside the human person.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T2 framing: external origin is not merely a theological assertion — it is encoded in the vocabulary.*

Singular in source (God), multiple in channel. Grace comes from God as the single source but is mediated through: direct divine bestowing (889-001, 888-001), the proclaimed word (Act 20:32), the forgiving act of another person (5470-002), the spirit poured out (Zec 12:10). Source singular; channels multiple.

*Adds precision: the source/channel distinction is not explicit in prior analysis.*

The source (God) does not change. The channel changes significantly. In human-to-human grace (forgiving, expressing), the immediate origin is the human person who extends it — but even this is derivative, patterned on and grounded in the divine forgiveness received (Eph 4:32). No context in the data shows grace originating independently of the divine source.

*Consistent with prior analysis.*

Yes. SD021 provides the most explicit model: divine disposition received at spirit level → relational standing created at soul level → specific capacity gifted into the person → character expressed outward through conduct and speech. The sb_classification "Spirit-soul interface" confirms movement across at least two constitutional levels.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD021). Adds T2 framing.*

The sequence (SD021): spirit-level reception → soul-level standing → capacity in the person → character expressed outward. Not reversible — outward expression does not produce spirit-level reception; it flows from it. However, SD029 (the grace-inference producing inner confidence) suggests cognitive engagement with grace can deepen the spirit-level reception — some bidirectional reinforcement within the sequence.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds note on possible bidirectional reinforcement — not previously named.*

Yes — multiple inner senses. **Inner sight**: the eye-formula of chen (SD041) — grace as being-seen by the one whose gaze constitutes standing. **Inner hearing**: SD012 (listen, Reg 213, 11 verses) — receptivity of hearing as the inner posture through which grace is received; the hardened heart refusing to hear is the condition that closes off grace. **Spiritual discernment**: Zec 12:10's grace-produced mourning suggests a discernment of what has been given that goes beyond the five inner senses.

*Adds structure: prior analysis addressed perception in individual SD pointers without examining it as faculty engagement. The prompt surfaces that grace engages at least three distinct inner senses — more perceptive coverage than most characteristics.*

Enables and deepens. Zec 12:10: grace outpoured produces the capacity to see ("when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced") — grace enables the inner sight that recognises what grace has cost. SD012: grace enables hearing-receptivity. The impairment is in the counter-condition: pride and hardened hearing impair perception, and the absence of grace may sustain that impairment.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds faculty framing.*

Grace is inherently perceptual in its operation — it requires the inner person to see, hear, and recognise what is being given. Grace that is not perceived is grace that does not land (SD043: acharistos). The perceptive faculty is integral, not incidental: grace requires recognition to complete its operation. This is why impaired perception simultaneously blocks reception — and why grace itself must first enable perception before it can be received.

*New finding as a T3-level statement: the integral relationship between grace and perception — requiring perception to complete its operation, and enabling that perception — is not previously stated in these terms.*

Yes. SD029 (Rom 8:32): grace produces inner confidence through reasoning — "he who did not spare his own Son... how will he not also graciously give us all things?" The inner-being state of confidence is derived by inference from the supreme grace-event. This is a cognitive act: the person thinks their way into the assurance that grace makes available.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD029). Adds T3 framing.*

Enables a specific form: the grace-inference (SD029). SD048 raises the question of whether right thinking about grace is itself grace-enabled — implying grace enhances rather than bypasses cognition — but this is not confirmed in the data.

*Gap identified: the relationship between grace and cognition is partially addressed but not fully examined.*

Grace produces a particular cognitive form — reasoned assurance, not merely experienced certainty. Grace can be the ground of inner confidence even when experiential feeling is absent. This distinguishes grace's cognitive engagement from purely affective reception.

*Adds precision: grace operating cognitively independently of affect is a significant finding not previously stated in these terms.*

Not directly addressed. However: SD029 (Rom 8:32 inference-structure): assurance derived from grace depends on remembering what God has done — the supreme grace-event functions as a remembered ground for current confidence. The lament-supplication tradition involves the creature appealing to God's prior acts of grace — a form of memory-grounded petition (SD005 and the weeping-supplication complex of SD005).

*Gap identified: memory faculty is implicitly engaged — particularly in the lament tradition and the grace-inference structure — but not directly addressed in the data.*

Yes — extensively. Immediate responses are all affective: fear (Luk 1:28-30), mourning (Zec 12:10), weeping (SD038), joy (charis/chairo root-sharing — SD020). Sustained effects include tenderheartedness (eusplanchnos, Eph 4:32 — SD030). The supplication posture is affectively charged: "deep need and relational vulnerability" (890-001 description). Grace and joy share a root (SD020) — grace is inherently joy-productive.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T3 framing: affect engaged at both the receiving end (fear, mourning, weeping) and the sustained state end (tenderheartedness, joy) — a complete affective arc.*

Enables and deepens. Zec 12:10: grace outpoured produces mourning — the capacity to grieve what grace has cost. SD020: charis/chairo root-sharing — grace is inherently joy-productive, the ground of joy as a sustained inner state. Eph 4:32: eusplanchnos — grace softens the inner person affectively, enabling compassion-feeling. Grace enables appropriate affect: fear, mourning, and joy and peace are all appropriate responses to different aspects of grace.

*Adds structure: the enabling of appropriate affect — the specific affects corresponding to each encounter with grace — is a significant T3 finding.*

Grace is affectively generative — it produces specific, appropriate affects rather than a generic emotional response. The sequence (fear → mourning → joy/peace, or prostration → questioning → gratitude) reveals that grace produces an arc of affective responses corresponding to the stages of recognition, reception, and inhabitation. Grace shapes the affective life of the person over time toward appropriate responsiveness.

*New finding as a T3-level statement: grace as affectively generative — producing a structured arc of affects, not a single emotion — is not previously named in these terms.*

Yes — the most theologically contested engagement in the data. SD010 (11 verses with will, Reg 173): explicitly identified as "the most theologically contested question the grace registry raises." SD034 (1Cor 15:10: "I worked harder than any of them, though it was not I, but the grace of God"): genuine human effort and grace-as-agent simultaneously true. SD033 (Eph 2:8: "not your own doing") establishes the limit of volitional contribution to grace-reception.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD010, SD034). Adds T3 framing: volitional engagement is explicitly the most contested question — T3.6 is a priority area for further examination.*

Not resolved. SD010 holds three possibilities open: grace operates with the will (enabling it to turn), upon the will (overriding volitional resistance), or alongside the will (grace as the condition within which the will operates). SD034 suggests the "with the will" model. SD026 (even supplication posture is grace-given) pushes toward the "upon the will" model. The tension is not resolved.

*Gap identified: the volition-grace relationship is the most contested and least resolved question in the data.*

**Design note:** The enable/deepen/bypass/impair options do not fully capture what happens with grace and volition. 1Cor 15:10 holds them in paradox — a sustained both/and that the inner person inhabits without resolution. A revision to the prompt option set is recommended: add "holds in paradox" or "operates in both directions simultaneously" as a valid answer option for this characteristic and potentially for the catalogue as a whole.

Grace and volition are not simply competitive or cooperative — the relationship is more complex than either model captures. The paradox of 1Cor 15:10 may be the most accurate description: not a resolved either/or but a sustained both/and.

Yes. Giving side: grace exercised through forgiving (5470-002), extending (888-004), expressing in speech (SD022) — all agency-requiring acts. Receiving side: 1Cor 15:10 — grace enables and animates the person's own agency ("I worked harder than any of them"). Grace does not suppress agency; it constitutes and energises it.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD034). Adds T3 framing.*

Enables and deepens. 1Cor 15:10: grace as the animating force enabling the person to work harder — not replacing their agency but being the power within which genuine effort occurs. Act 20:32 (grace building up): agency-enhancing function. The forgiving act (5470-002) requires and expresses full agency.

*Consistent with prior analysis.*

Grace is agency-constituting, not agency-replacing. The person who receives grace becomes more fully agentive — able to work, forgive, extend, speak graciously — not less. This distinguishes grace from deterministic models that would make it the suppression of human agency.

*Adds T3 framing: agency-constituting character of grace is a clear finding not previously named as a T3-level faculty observation.*

Yes. Grace operates in direct relationship with moral evaluation — it is the characteristic that operates precisely where moral evaluation has concluded the person has no standing. The guilt registry (10 co-occurring verses) and the moral failure cluster (SD007: evil 12, sin 6, transgression 6) locate grace in proximity to moral failure. Grace presupposes the moral evaluation has produced a verdict — and then operates regardless of that verdict.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD007). Adds T3 framing: moral evaluation is engaged as the pre-condition that grace transcends.*

Neither bypassing nor impairing. Grace operates after moral evaluation, not instead of it. SD047 (justice and grace, 7 verses): holding both simultaneously requires a particular moral evaluation capacity. Grace may deepen moral evaluation by making the person more acutely aware of what they have received that they did not deserve.

*Gap identified: the relationship between grace and moral evaluation is touched but not directly examined.*

Grace produces moral clarity — sharpening rather than blunting moral awareness. The person who recognises grace has made a moral evaluation: they know they have received what they did not deserve. Grace sharpens rather than bypasses the moral evaluation faculty.

*New finding as a T3-level statement: grace as producing moral clarity is not previously stated in these terms.*

Yes. SD007 (moral failure cluster: grace appearing most fully where the creature's moral condition is most exposed) and guilt co-occurrence (10 verses) establish that grace operates in the vicinity of the conscience. SD026 (Zec 12:10: the spirit of grace producing mourning when the pierced one is seen) suggests grace operates upon the conscience at its most acute — the recognition of what one's sin has cost is both the work of conscience and the first response to the full gift of grace.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T3 framing: conscience is engaged as the faculty through which grace's cost is recognised.*

Deepens and enables. SD026 (Zec 12:10): the spirit of grace produces mourning — conscience at its deepest: not merely moral verdict but grief at the relational and personal cost. Grace enables the conscience to feel fully without destroying the person — because what the conscience reveals is held within the grace simultaneously given. Grace makes safe space for conscience to do its fullest work.

*New finding as a T3-level statement: grace as the condition within which conscience can operate fully without destroying the person is not previously stated in these terms.*

Grace and conscience are not opposed but mutually necessary. The deepest encounters with grace are moments of deepest conscience-awareness. Grace does not anaesthetise conscience — it enables it. And conscience, fully awakened, is the inner organ through which grace is most deeply received.

*New finding: the mutual necessity of grace and conscience — each requiring the other for fullest operation — is a significant T3 observation not previously stated.*

Yes. Eph 4:32 (5470-002): "Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you." The person integrates moral awareness (we have received forgiveness), volition (choose to forgive), and action (the forgiving act itself) — conscientiousness as the integrated response. The grace-agency paradox (SD034: 1Cor 15:10) also engages conscientiousness: working hard while holding the grace that works through you.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T3 framing.*

Enables and produces — but through a mediating inner state. SD030 (Eph 4:32): grace received → tenderheartedness produced → kindness and forgiveness extended. Grace produces the inner conditions from which conscientiousness flows; it is not a direct production but a mediated one. The inner state (grace received, tenderheartedness) generates the integrated outward response.

*Adds precision: grace produces conscientiousness through the mediating inner state of tenderheartedness.*

Grace is the constitutive ground of moral integration. The person formed by grace forgives not from calculation or discipline but because they have been forgiven, are tenderhearted, and that inner condition naturally integrates into a forgiving act. Grace produces moral coherence: inner state and outward act correspond, both arising from the same received reality.

*New finding: grace as constitutive ground of moral integration is not previously named as a T3 observation.*

Yes — this is the primary faculty engagement of grace. Grace is constitutively relational (T1.8): it requires a giver and receiver, creates standing between persons, operates across the divine-human and human-human interfaces. Relational capacity is the faculty through which all of grace's operation is mediated.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T3 framing: relational capacity is not merely engaged — it is the primary faculty home of grace.*

Enables and deepens. The person who has received grace can extend grace to others (5470-002), stand in gracious relational disposition (889-003), and approach God in supplication (890-001). The tenderheartedness (eusplanchnos, Eph 4:32) produced by grace deepens relational capacity — the inner softening enabling genuine connection and response to others' need.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T3 framing.*

Relational capacity is the faculty most constitutively shaped by grace. The person formed by grace becomes more relationally capable. Grace is the primary relational faculty-developer in the programme's inner-being landscape — at least at the level of this registry's evidence.

*Adds T3-level statement: prior analysis addressed relational effects of grace; the prompt names grace as the primary relational faculty developer — a more structural framing.*

Extensively — this is the primary and most evidenced direction. Group 888-001 (22 relevant verses), 889-001 (15 relevant verses), 5471-001 (2 verses) all name the God-to-human direction as primary. Anchor verses: Eph 2:8, Rom 8:32, Exo 33:17, Luk 1:28.

*Consistent with prior analysis.*

Not the recipient's merit (Eph 2:8); not the recipient's strength (2Cor 12:9: sufficient in weakness); grounded in divine character and purpose (Exo 33:17: "I know you by name" — prior divine knowing); grounded in the supreme sacrifice (Rom 8:32: he who did not spare his own Son). The basis is the giver's character and prior knowing, not the receiver's qualification. Grace operates in covenantal context (889-001: toward those God knows by name) — directed, even if uncaused by merit.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing: the basis is the giver's character plus prior knowing — neither merit-based nor arbitrary.*

Free giving at personal cost (Rom 8:32); knowing individuals by name and giving from that knowing (Exo 33:17); giving beyond what the creature can request (Eph 2:7: "immeasurable riches"); extending toward the dead and incapacitated, not only the guilty (SD008); giving even the capacity to receive (SD026). The disposition: sovereign generosity grounded in personal knowledge, uncaused by the recipient's merit, directed toward the person's constitution as recipient.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing.*

Yes — through the supplication vocabulary (890-001: tachanum, 18 active verses). Dan 9:3 (anchor): earnest supplication with fasting and sackcloth. Zec 12:10 (anchor): pleas for mercy poured out by the spirit of grace. The human-to-God direction of grace is the supplication posture — the creature's appeal for what it cannot supply.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing: the human-to-God direction of grace is supplication specifically — not worship or thanksgiving generically, but the appeal of the dependent creature to the gracious source.*

Deep need and relational vulnerability (890-001: "the plea for mercy that arises from deep need and relational vulnerability before God"). Somatic forms confirm: prostration (body enacting dependence), fasting (renouncing self-sufficiency), sackcloth and ashes (self-lowering). SD001: "the body enacts what the inner person is doing — the whole person brought into alignment with the inner posture of plea." The required posture is total creaturely self-lowering before the gracious source.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing.*

The creature's constitutional dependence: the person approaches God in supplication having recognised they have no basis other than grace for the approach. SD035 (Rom 5:2 prosagōgē — access, approach): grace is the relational condition that transforms the threshold from impassable to open. The human-to-God direction of grace is the creature entering through the door that grace has opened.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing: prosagōgē as the relational access-concept is significant.*

Yes. Group 5470-002 (charizō as human forgiving act — 8 relevant verses): Eph 4:32 (anchor): "forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you." Group 889-003 (chen as gracious character quality): Pro 31:30 (anchor). Speech-grace (SD022: gracious words, speech seasoned with grace) as a further giving mode.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing.*

One must have received before genuinely giving. Eph 4:32: "as God in Christ forgave you" — the pattern is the divine gift received, and the human extension is grounded in that receipt. SD030 (eusplanchnos as mediating state): grace received → tenderheartedness produced → kindness and forgiveness extended. Grace extended from pride or self-righteousness is not grace in the structural sense.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD030). Adds T4 framing.*

They must have received grace (foundational reception); become tenderhearted (the mediating inner transformation — eusplanchnos); and passed through recognition of their own need (conscience-awareness, mourning) that makes giving genuine rather than condescending. The giver must be also a receiver who knows they are a receiver.

*Adds precision: the three conditions (received grace, tenderheartedness, recognition of own need) as a named sequence for the human giving-direction — not previously stated in these terms.*

Primarily God-to-human receiving is evidenced. Ruth 2:10 shows Ruth receiving favour from Boaz — a human-to-human grace event where Ruth is the receiver. The data addresses the receiving side primarily in the context of divine grace; human-to-human receiving is present but less developed.

*Gap identified: the inner conditions for receiving grace from another human person are not separately examined.*

By inference: the same conditions blocking reception of divine grace likely block reception of human grace — pride, merit-logic, ingratitude. Ruth 2:10 shows a reception-event: surprise, awareness of undeservingness, genuine openness. But systematic examination of human-to-human grace reception is not in the current data.

*Gap identified.*

Both are evidenced. Within existing bonds: covenantal grace of chen in 889-001 (favour toward those God knows by name); Eph 4:32 (forgiving one another — within the Christian community). Across boundaries: Ruth 2:10 is paradigmatic — grace crossing ethnic, social, and covenantal boundaries (Ruth as a Moabite receiving favour from an Israelite). The cross-boundary operation is one of grace's most distinctive features.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T4 framing.*

Both — and the cross-covenantal operation is theologically significant. The CHEN root's operation across Israelite and non-Israelite contexts (Gen 6:8: Noah; Gen 19:19: Lot; Ruth 2:10: Ruth) establishes that grace is not covenantally bounded even in the OT corpus. Luk 1:28-30 and Eph 2:8 extend this further in the NT.

*Adds T4 framing: cross-covenantal operation is established in the OT, not merely a NT development.*

The scope of grace is determined by the giver's character, not the recipient's relational position. Grace operates toward those who have no basis for it — foreigners, the dead, the morally failed, the incapacitated. The relational scope is: anyone who is in need and without basis — which is, structurally, every creature before God.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD008, SD032). Adds T4 framing.*

Luk 1:28 (anchor: 5471-001): the angelic announcement of grace to Mary — the angel Gabriel as messenger through whom the grace-bestowal is announced. This is angelic communication of grace — the closest evidence. Adversarial spiritual beings: not addressed.

*Gap identified: angelic mediation in the announcement of grace is present but not examined as a pattern. Adversarial activity not addressed.*

Yes — emphatically. 1Cor 15:10: "by the grace of God I am what I am" — identity-constituting transformation. Eph 2:1-5 (SD008): from dead to alive — the most radical condition-change in the corpus. 2Cor 12:9: not condition-change (weakness remains) but orientation-change (weakness-as-theatre-of-grace rather than weakness-as-problem). Both condition-change and orientation-change are evidenced.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD008, SD032). Adds T5 framing: both types present — unusually complete.*

The data suggests irreversibility for the deepest transformations. charitoō (Luk 1:28: kecharitōmenē) is a perfect passive — completed act with continuing state. 1Cor 15:10 is framed in terms of being ("I am what I am") — implying a permanent condition. However, grace "received in vain" (2Cor 6:1, referenced in SD pointers) suggests not every grace-reception produces irreversible transformation. The question is not fully resolved.

*Gap identified: the reversibility question is partially addressed but not resolved.*

Yes — multiple sequences. SD031 (Luk 1:28-30): grace-announcement → fear → reassurance → receptivity. SD038 (Zec 12:10): grace outpoured → recognition of the pierced one → mourning → weeping → supplication. SD021 (charis four faces): divine disposition received → standing established → capacity gifted → character expressed. SD030 (Eph 4:32): grace received → tenderheartedness produced → kindness and forgiveness extended. All sequences share a common structure: something received → inner transformation → outward expression.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T5 framing: multiple sequences converging on a common structure.*

The sequences together reveal a common pattern: encounter with grace → creaturely recognition response (fear, mourning, prostration) → inner transformation (tenderheartedness, standing, capacity) → outward expression (forgiving, gracious speech, ministry). The creaturely recognition response is consistently present as the transitional state — the inner moment of registering the weight of what is given — before transformation settles. Grace does not bypass the person's recognition; it requires and produces it as part of the sequence.

*New finding: the creaturely recognition response as a consistent transitional state in all grace-sequences is not previously named as a T5 observation. A significant structural finding.*

Multiple mechanisms evidenced: (1) **Encounter** — change through a specific relational event (Luk 1:28; Ruth 2:10); (2) **Spirit-outpouring** — the Spirit of grace poured out (Zec 12:10); (3) **Word** — grace operating through the proclaimed word as a constructive mechanism (Act 20:32); (4) **Inference** — change through the cognitive act of reasoning about what has already happened (SD029, Rom 8:32); (5) **Mediated transformation** — grace producing an intermediate inner state (tenderheartedness) that then flows outward (SD030, Eph 4:32).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T5 framing: five distinct mechanisms — encounter, outpouring, word, inference, mediated-transformation. Unusual richness for a single characteristic.*

Yes. Sovereign bestowal uses encounter/outpouring; formative development uses the word-mechanism (Act 20:32); cognitive assurance uses the inference mechanism (Rom 8:32); relational extending uses tenderheartedness-mediation (Eph 4:32). The mechanism adapts to the mode of grace's operation.

*Adds precision: prior analysis noted individual mechanisms without distinguishing them by context.*

Yes. 2Cor 12:9: "my grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." Grace inhabits affliction — not resolving it but operating within it. SD032: "grace does not resolve weakness — it inhabits and operates within it. The inner-being transformation grace produces is not from weakness to strength but from weakness-as-problem to weakness-as-theatre-of-grace." SD008 (Eph 2:1-5): grace addressing the dead — the most extreme condition of incapacity.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD032, SD008). Adds T5 framing.*

Grace inhabits suffering — it does not respond from outside. SD032: weakness is the context in which grace's power is most visible. Suffering does not produce grace — but is the primary context in which grace's distinctive character is most fully revealed. Suffering is revealing, not productive, of grace.

*Adds precision: the distinction between suffering as revealing (not producing) grace is a significant T5 observation not previously stated in these terms.*

Yes. Act 20:32: "the word of his grace, which is able to build you up" — oikodomeō (to build, construct) as the formation mechanism. 1Cor 15:10: grace constituting identity over time. Eph 4:32 sequence — a pattern repeated over time produces character. The gracious disposition of 889-003 (gracious character quality expressed in conduct and wisdom) is the fruit of formation by grace.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T5 framing.*

Grace is not merely a stage in the formative arc — it is its animating principle. 1Cor 15:10: "it was not I, but the grace of God that is with me" — grace is the agent within formation, not its recipient. The person participates genuinely (they work harder) but grace is what works through them. Grace is the formative power, not merely a characteristic the person develops.

*Adds precision: grace as formative agent rather than formative product is a significant T5 distinction. Prior analysis noted it in SD034 without drawing it as a T5-level finding.*

Yes. Eph 2:7 (SD027): "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us." Rom 5:2 (SD005): "we rejoice in hope of the glory of God" — standing-in-grace carries a forward orientation toward glory.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD027, SD005). Adds T5 framing.*

The "immeasurable riches" (Eph 2:7) — a fullness that the present experience is real but limited. Present grace is the deposit and foretaste; eschatological grace is the completion. SD021's sequential model implies that the full constitutional movement of grace will be complete and unimpeded in the eschatological state.

*Adds precision: the eschatological fullness completes the constitutional movement described in T2.10 and SD021.*

From Section 5 of the data: seeking (R140: 40 verses), strength (R187: 15), love (R103: 14), desire (R043: 12), evil (R057: 12), will (R173: 11), calling (R019: 11), listen (R213: 11), peace (R117: 10), might (R198: 10), faith (R059: 10), guilt (R073: 10).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T6 framing.*

Several structural signals: **Seeking at 40 verses** is the dominant signal — the creature seeking grace is the most common relational configuration in the corpus (SD002). **Moral failure cluster** (evil 12, guilt 10, sin 6, transgression 6) confirms grace appears most fully where moral failure is most present. **Power cluster** (strength 15, might 10) confirms the 2Cor 12:9 structural connection (SD003). **Love at 14** as the strongest within-cluster signal raises the primary boundary question (SD013). The pattern reveals grace at the intersection of divine initiative and creaturely need.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T6 framing: the co-occurrence pattern as a whole reveals grace's structural position.*

Multiple sequences. Grace **precedes**: faith (Eph 2:8: grace as sphere, faith as channel — SD006); repentance (SD003: Zec 12:10 — grace precedes and enables the turn); supplication (SD026: grace produces even the capacity for appeal). Grace **follows**: moral failure and guilt (consistently appears after recognition of failure); mourning (in SD038, mourning follows grace outpoured). Grace **accompanies**: hope (Rom 5:2 — SD005); calling (SD011).

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T6 framing: the sequential relationships are complex — grace precedes some characteristics and follows others.*

Primarily causal: grace precedes and produces faith, repentance, supplication, mourning (SD003, SD006, SD026, SD038) — not merely correlational. SD028 (Dan 9:23: God's response preceded the completion of Daniel's plea) suggests the relationship can be simultaneous rather than sequential. The boundary between grace preceding and grace accompanying is not always clear.

*Adds precision: the causal character of grace's relationships is a significant T6 finding.*

Yes — multiple. Grace produces: seeking/supplication (SD026); mourning (SD038); tenderheartedness (SD030); joy (SD020 root-sharing); assurance/confidence (SD029); identity (1Cor 15:10 — SD004). Grace is highly generative — it produces a wide range of characteristics in the person who receives it.

*Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T6 framing: the range of what grace produces is unusual.*

The data raises this through the CHEN root family (SD024): supplication (tachanum) is the receiving side of the same phenomenon — possibly a constituent element of the grace-complex. SD018 suggests forgiveness may be a subspecies of grace (charizō carrying both senses lexically). The SD013 grace-love question raises whether grace is a constituent of a larger love-disposition or a distinct characteristic. Not resolved.

*Gap identified: constitutive relationships are raised but not resolved.*

Yes — extensively. Term sharing ratio: 0.846 (11 of 13 terms shared). CHEN root family shared across pray (Reg 212, 10 shared Hebrew terms), mercy (Reg 111), kindness (Reg 99), forgiveness (Reg 64). charizō shared between grace and forgiveness. charis shared with rejoicing (Reg 132 — charis/chairo root connection — SD020).

*Consistent with prior analysis. High sharing ratio (0.846) is a structural signal — grace is lexically central to the C17 cluster.*

Yes. charis/chairo (G5485/G5463) root-sharing with rejoicing (SD020): characteristics sharing the charis root share a conceptual relationship to free, joyful giving. CHEN root family (SD024): the most complete root architecture in the OT vocabulary, spanning the full grace-circuit.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD020, SD024). Adds T6 framing.*

The high sharing ratio and root-level connections reveal that grace is lexically embedded within a cluster of conceptually related characteristics — not self-contained but constitutively linked to mercy, kindness, forgiveness, prayer, and rejoicing. Grace is the generative centre of the C17 relational cluster.

*Consistent with prior analysis (SD014: C17 synthesis question). Adds T6 framing.*

Love (Reg 103, 14 co-occurring verses) is the most proximate — SD013 raises the boundary question but does not resolve it. SD025 proposes chen initiates / chesed sustains as a potential distinction within the grace-kindness pairing. The precise distinction between grace and love remains open.

*Gap identified: the grace-love boundary is the most important distinction question and is unresolved. A T6 gap requiring Session D.*

SD025 proposal (chen initiates, chesed sustains) suggests a distinction of sequential function. The grace-love question may be a distinction of specificity (grace as uncaused love, love as the broader disposition). Both are proposals, not confirmed findings.

*Gap identified.*

Yes. Section 6 of the data: Pro 31:30 (shared with praise/R121); Zec 12:10 (shared with mourning/R113); Rom 5:2 (shared with hope/R78); 2Cor 12:9 (shared with pride/R123); Eph 2:8 (shared with debauchery/R39 and yielding/R180); Eph 4:32 (shared with heart/R183 and kindness/R99). Six shared anchor verses across seven other characteristics.

*Consistent with prior analysis.*

Each shared anchor encodes a structural relationship: Zec 12:10 + mourning: the grace-mourning sequence (SD038) — deepest grace-response is grief. Rom 5:2 + hope: grace and hope co-constituting the same inner standing (SD005). 2Cor 12:9 + pride: grace-in-weakness as structural antithesis of pride (SD004).
