The heart (T2) is where the integrating functions of willing, knowing, moral evaluation, and affect operate (T3.6 volition, T3.2 cognition, T3.8 moral evaluation, T3.4 affect). The mind (T2) is where cognition (T3.2) and memory (T3.3) operate. The soul-totality (T2) corresponds to the conscientiousness and relational capacity (T3.10, T3.11) that engage the whole person. The constitutional location and the faculty operation are two descriptions of the same inner reality: location is where the faculty is housed; faculty is what the location does. The new covenant writes on the heart/mind (T2) and thereby transforms cognition toward knowing God (T3.2) — the constitutional location is the mechanism for the faculty transformation.

T2 reveals that covenant operates constitutionally in the heart, mind, soul, mouth, and body. T4 reveals that covenant operates relationally through God-to-human, human-to-God, and horizontal interfaces. The T2 × T4 synthesis: the constitutional locations and the relational interfaces are not separate registers — they are the same inner reality viewed from different angles. The soul's engagement in covenant (T2 — "with all his heart and all his soul") is precisely the inner posture required for genuine human-to-God movement (T4.2 — Q&A-106). The mouth as constitutional location (T2 — Isa 59:21) is the organ through which the human-to-God relational direction is enacted (oath as speech act — Q&A-024, T1.4). The blood as somatic covenant-medium (T2 — Q&A-053) is the mediating substance through which the God-to-human interface is constituted (the blood of the covenant — Q&A-101). Constitutional location and relational interface are two views of the same inner-being dynamic.

T2 identifies a shift in constitutional origin across the covenant trajectory: from external bestowal (Noahic) to bilateral reception (Sinai) to generational transmission (Davidic) to internal Spirit-generation (new covenant — Q&A-064). T5 identifies a corresponding shift in the mechanism of change: from external-demand (old covenant letter) to internal-inscription (new covenant Spirit — Q&A-131, Q&A-132). The T2 × T5 synthesis: the constitutional origin shift (T2) and the mechanism shift (T5) are the same development described at two different levels. The new covenant's internal Spirit-generation (T2) is the constitutional reality that underlies the internal-inscription mechanism (T5). These are not two separate developments but one single inner-being transformation described at the constitutional-location level (T2) and at the developmental-mechanism level (T5). The new covenant produces a change in where covenant comes from (T2: now from within through the Spirit) and a change in how it produces change (T5: now through inscription rather than instruction).

T2 establishes that the heart is the primary constitutional location of covenant, with body (blood, circumcision) as the somatic deposit. T6 reveals that covenant's deepest structural relationship is with faithfulness (lexically co-generated through the emunah-amanah root — Q&A-157). The T2 × T6 synthesis: the constitutional deposit of covenant (bodily circumcision, generational word-transmission — Q&A-059) is the constitutional mechanism through which covenant's structural relationships are transmitted to new participants. Covenant's bond with faithfulness (T6) is transmitted across generations through the bodily sign (circumcision) and the spoken word (Isa 59:21) — the somatic deposit (T2) is the mechanism of structural relationship preservation (T6). The body carries the covenant's relational structure forward through time.

T2 identifies blood and circumcision as the primary somatic covenant locations. T7 establishes the lexical arc showing covenant as simultaneously relational, bodily, oral, legal, moral, transformative, and eschatological. The T2 × T7 synthesis: the bodily dimension identified in T2 is confirmed and deepened by the lexical analysis of T7. The root meaning of karat (to cut — Q&A-170) and the blood-of-the-covenant vocabulary are etymologically embedded in the bodily-somatic dimension of the covenant vocabulary arc. The LXX translation choice (diathēkē — Q&A-177) adds the death/inheritance dimension to the somatic vocabulary: the blood is the means of mediation, and the testator's death is required for the testament to take effect. T2's somatic findings are not incidental features but are inscribed in the etymology and semantic development of the vocabulary itself (T7).

T3 reveals that the inner faculties covenant most deeply engages are Other-oriented (relational capacity, conscience, moral evaluation, memory). T4 reveals that covenant's relational interfaces are asymmetric (God initiates; human responds; horizontal is grounded in vertical). The T3 × T4 synthesis: the Other-oriented faculty bias (T3) and the asymmetric relational structure (T4) are coherently related — the inner faculties that covenant engages are precisely those required for the kind of asymmetric responsive relationship that covenant constitutes. The relational capacity (T3.11) is the constitutional equipment for genuine connection; the covenant's relational interfaces (T4) are the actual connections that this capacity enables. The inner posture required for human-to-God covenant movement (T4.2 — Q&A-106: reverent fear, love, all-heart, hearing orientation) corresponds directly to the inner faculties that covenant engages: fear engages moral evaluation (T3.8), love engages affect and relational capacity (T3.4, T3.11), all-heart engages volition (T3.6), and hearing engages perception (T3.1). T3 and T4 are two analytical views of the same responsive inner-being structure.

T3 identifies the new covenant's faculty-level effect as the transformation of volition (from called-for to liberated — Q&A-085) and the production of covenantal knowing (Q&A-073). T5 identifies the developmental sequence through which this transformation occurs (failure → inscription → belonging → knowing — Q&A-128) and the mechanism change (letter to Spirit — Q&A-131). The T3 × T5 synthesis: the faculty transformations identified in T3 (volition liberated, cognition/knowing produced) are the outcome of the developmental sequence and mechanism identified in T5. The sequence "inscription → belonging → knowing" (T5) describes how the specific faculty transformations of T3 are produced. The mechanism shift from letter to Spirit (T5) is the developmental mechanism that produces the volitional liberation (T3.6) and cognitive knowing (T3.2) that the new covenant delivers. T5 is the developmental story; T3 is the faculty-level outcome of that story.

T3 reveals that covenant is form; relational capacity is content (Q&A-100 — the T3.11 reciprocal finding). T6 reveals that covenant is structurally prior to and produces other characteristics including hope, knowing, and faithfulness. The T3 × T5 synthesis: the T3.11 finding (covenant as form; relational capacity as content) generalises to the T6 finding (covenant as architecturally prior to its C17 neighbour characteristics). In both cases, covenant is the structure; other inner-being realities (relational capacity, love/hesed, hope, knowing) are the content that the structure enables and sustains. This generalisation confirms that covenant's architectural character applies at both the faculty level (T3) and the inter-characteristic level (T6) — it is structurally prior at every level of the inner-being analysis.

T3 shows that memory is one of the most structurally prominent inner faculties in covenant — divine memory of covenant is the guarantee of its continuing operation; human memory is the activating condition for faithfulness (Q&A-074, Q&A-076). T7 shows that the primary verse evidence spans four literary forms (legal-narrative, prophetic oracle, epistle-argument, liturgical — Q&A-181), all of which are forms that require and shape memory. The T3 × T7 synthesis: the memory-faculty centrality (T3) and the literary form analysis (T7) converge on the same insight: covenant is constitutively a narrative and liturgical reality — it is held active through storytelling, declaration, and ritual re-enactment. The Eucharistic "do this in remembrance of me" (Q&A-075) is the liturgical instantiation of memory's role in covenant (T3), and it is carried by the liturgical literary form (T7). Memory and liturgy are co-constituting — covenant is kept alive through the practice of remembering performed in narrative and liturgical forms.

T4 reveals that the relational scope of covenant expands progressively from particular (Abraham) to national (Israel) to universal (new covenant in Christ — Q&A-119). T5 reveals that covenant moves toward eschatological fullness — from the eternal covenant (Heb 13:20 — Q&A-140) to the new creation (Rev 21:1-5 — Q&A-141). The T4 × T5 synthesis: the expanding relational scope (T4) and the eschatological trajectory (T5) are the same movement described in two registers. The Noahic covenant's universal scope (all living creatures) is the anticipation of the new covenant's universal offer and ultimately the eschatological new creation's universal scope. Covenant moves simultaneously toward wider relational inclusion (T4) and toward deeper fullness of the relationship (T5). The two trajectories are not separate but co-directional — the expansion of who is included (T4) and the deepening of what is included in (T5) are aspects of the same eschatological movement.

T4 reveals that God-to-human covenant operates through five modes (establishment, faithfulness, inscription, oath-binding, mediation — Q&A-101). T6 reveals that covenant is causally prior to other characteristics and produces love/hesed, hope, knowing, and faithfulness as its effects (Q&A-152). The T4 × T5 synthesis: the God-to-human directional modes (T4) are the relational mechanisms through which the causal productivity identified in T6 operates. God establishes covenant (T4.1) → covenant becomes the ground for hope (T6, Q&A-150, Q&A-152). God inscribes on the heart (T4.1 mode 3) → knowing is produced (T6, Q&A-152). God mediates through Christ (T4.1 mode 5) → sanctification is produced (T6, Q&A-152). The five God-to-human modes (T4) are the directional mechanisms that produce the T6 causal effects. T4 is the directional 'how'; T6 is the consequential 'what'.

T4 establishes that covenant's relational interfaces are carried through specific acts — oath-taking, blood-shedding, covenant-meal participation, circumcision. T7 establishes that these acts are carried by specific literary forms — legal-narrative (covenant-making), prophetic oracle (new covenant promise), liturgical (Eucharist), and epistle-argument (Hebrews). The T4 × T7 synthesis: the relational acts of covenant (T4) are embedded in and shaped by their literary contexts (T7). The covenantal meal (T4 receiving — Q&A-113) is not merely a relational act but a liturgical-formular act (T7). The prophetic oracle of the new covenant (Jer 31:31-34) is not merely a T4 divine-to-human act of declaration but is embedded in the prophetic genre's authority structures (T7). The literary form analysis (T7) provides the interpretive framework within which the relational interface data (T4) must be read. Responsible interpretation of the covenantal acts requires attention to their literary embedding.

T5 identifies the developmental sequence of covenant's inner-being transformation: failure → divine initiative → inscription → belonging → knowing (Q&A-128) and the mechanism shift from external-demand to internal-inscription (Q&A-131). T6 identifies the structural relationships: covenant produces knowing, hope, faithfulness, and freedom; and is structurally prior to other C17 characteristics. The T5 × T6 synthesis: the developmental sequence (T5) is the mechanism through which the structural productivity (T6) operates. Covenant produces knowing (T6 — Q&A-152) through the developmental sequence of inscription → knowing (T5 — Q&A-128). Covenant produces hope (T6) through the sequential logic of covenant-inclusion-as-ground-of-hope (T5 eschatological trajectory — Q&A-140). The developmental story (T5) explains how the structural relationships (T6) come to be — T5 is the causal mechanism; T6 is the structural outcome. The mechanism shift from letter to Spirit (T5) is the developmental explanation for why the new covenant is structurally prior to the characteristics it enables (T6) — the new covenant's internal-inscription mechanism makes it the constitutive source of inner-being transformation, not merely one input among equals.

T5 establishes covenant's eschatological orientation — the eternal covenant (Heb 13:20 — Q&A-140) and the new creation as the covenant's telos (Q&A-141). T7 establishes that the full vocabulary arc includes an eschatological dimension (Q&A-179) and that the primary anchor verse (Jer 31:33 / Heb 8:10 — Q&A-185) contains the covenant's telos within its text (the knowing of God — Jer 31:34). The T5 × T7 synthesis: the eschatological trajectory (T5) is encoded in the vocabulary and in the primary anchor verse itself (T7). The eternal covenant (T5) is named in the Hebrews vocabulary (T7 — Heb 13:20 as anchor). The eschatological knowing of God (T5 — Q&A-141: fully realised eschatological knowing) is anticipated in the primary anchor verse's promise "they shall all know me" (T7 — Q&A-185). T5's developmental trajectory finds its lexical and evidential home in the specific texts that T7 identifies as the evidential foundation. The eschatological dimension is not imposed on the data but is resident in the vocabulary arc and primary anchor verse.

T6 establishes that covenant and faithfulness are lexically co-generated through the emunah-amanah root (Q&A-157, Q&A-158) and that covenant is structurally central in the inner-being landscape (Q&A-147). T7 confirms through the vocabulary arc analysis that covenant encompasses seven simultaneous semantic dimensions (Q&A-179). The T6 × T7 synthesis: the co-occurrence hub status and structural priority of covenant (T6) is grounded in the breadth and depth of the vocabulary arc (T7). Covenant occupies a hub position in the co-occurrence data (T6) because its vocabulary spans seven dimensions (T7) — a characteristic whose terms appear across the relational, moral, transformative, bodily, and eschatological registers will naturally co-occur with the widest range of other inner-being characteristics. The structural centrality of covenant (T6) is the co-occurrence consequence of its semantic comprehensiveness (T7). The two findings confirm each other: wide vocabulary arc → wide co-occurrence → structural hub position. Covenant's place at the centre of the inner-being landscape (T6) is not arbitrary but lexically grounded (T7).

T1's five-mode structure maps precisely onto T2's constitutional levels: existential mode (soul/nephesh), companionship mode (heart), desire-driven mode (desire-faculty), suffering mode (somatic), ontological mode (spirit — inferential). Definition and constitutional location are co-determining: the mode of fellowship determines the constitutional level at which it operates. The T1 definition (object determines character) is confirmed by T2's constitutional evidence — the desire-faculty as the constitutional engine of fellowship direction means desire determines the object, and the object determines which constitutional level is most engaged.

T1 establishes that fellowship is object-determined and progressively constitutional; T3 shows that fellowship shapes every inner faculty in conformity with its object. These are two descriptions of the same reality: "object determines character" (T1) is the principle; "every faculty shaped by fellowship direction" (T3) is the mechanism. The five-mode structure (T1) explains why T3 finds diverse faculty effects: existential mode primarily engages affect and relational capacity; companionship mode primarily engages volition, moral evaluation, and conscientiousness; suffering mode primarily engages affect and agency; ontological mode primarily engages relational capacity at its deepest level.

T1's five modes correspond directly to T4's relational directions: existential mode = all-the-living (widest scope); companionship mode = human-to-human (giving and receiving); covenantal mode = covenant partner; ontological mode = human-to-God (deepest). The scope-narrows-as-depth-increases finding (T4, Q&A-119) is the relational expression of T1's definition: as the fellowship object becomes more specific (from all-the-living to divine nature), the depth increases while the scope narrows. T1's structural negative (misdirected fellowship) maps onto T4's receiving-direction analysis (Hos 4:17 — prior wrong fellowship blocks reception of right fellowship).

T1 establishes that habitual fellowship produces constitutional character formation; T5 maps this as the primary formation mechanism and identifies the sequence (volitional alignment → habituation → constitutional fixing). Together T1 and T5 reveal the full arc: the definitional claim that fellowship's object determines character (T1) is the endpoint of a formation process that moves through distinct phases (T5). The five-mode structure (T1) generates two distinct formation mechanisms in T5: participatory-transfer (companionship → habituation → character) and wound-as-cleansing (suffering → inner purification). T1's most radical mode (ontological, 2Pe 1:4) is T5's eschatological telos (divine nature participation as the formation endpoint).

T1's five-mode structure explains T6's co-occurrence pattern. The desire-R43 co-occurrence (6 shared verses — strongest signal) reflects T1's finding that desire is the internal motivating state directing fellowship — they co-occur because desire directs fellowship. The unity-R167 co-occurrence reflects T1's finding that fellowship is the participatory inner reality of which unity is the corporate outcome — they co-occur because fellowship produces unity. The structural connections in T6 are the cross-registry expression of T1's definitional relationships.

T1's five-mode structure is encoded in the vocabulary's semantic arc (T7): existential → companionship → moral complicity → suffering → ontological maps precisely onto the semantic range (Ecc 9:4 → Psa 119:63 → Mat 23:30 → Phili 3:10 → 2Pe 1:4). The vocabulary is not a loose collection but a precisely structured arc corresponding to the definitional five modes. T7's grammatical analysis confirms T1's dispositional/act/constitutional distinction: the Hithpael (self-joining act) corresponds to T1's active dimension; the Pual (being-joined condition — Hos 4:17) corresponds to T1's constitutional dimension; the adjectival terms (H2270 companion, H2278 consort) correspond to T1's dispositional dimension.

T2's constitutional locations map directly onto T3's faculty engagements. The desire-faculty location (T2.5) corresponds to T3.6 volition and T3.7 agency — desire and will are structurally adjacent in directing fellowship. The heart location (T2.3) corresponds to T3.8 moral evaluation, T3.9 conscience, T3.10 conscientiousness — the heart-level constitutional engagement with fellowship produces the moral-faculty effects. The soul-level (T2.2 — Ecc 9:4) corresponds to T3.4 affect — the existential fellowship produces hope as an affective state at the soul level. Constitutional location determines which faculties are primarily engaged.

T2's constitutional origin analysis (multiple origins — divine initiative, human volitional alignment, existential given) directly maps onto T4's relational directions: divine initiative (God-to-human direction — T4.1); human volitional alignment (human-to-God and human-giving — T4.2, T4.3); existential given (the condition of all-the-living — T4.5 scope analysis). The constitutional origin determines the relational direction: the mode of fellowship's origin determines who is the primary relational agent in that mode.

T2's constitutional map and T5's formative account describe the same reality from space and time perspectives. The desire-faculty (T2.5) as constitutional engine maps onto T5's formation sequence beginning point (volitional alignment — desire-directed). The heart-level formation (T2.3 — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23) is T5's constitutional-formation endpoint. The body-deposit question (T2.8) opens into T5.7's developmental consequence analysis — the wound-mark as a formative trace raises the question of whether suffering-fellowship leaves a permanent constitutional marker, a question that remains open pending Session D.

T2's desire-faculty location (H2266 and desire vocabulary — 6-verse co-occurrence with R43) explains why desire (R43) is the strongest co-occurrence signal in T6. The co-occurrence is constitutionally grounded — desire and fellowship operate at the same constitutional level (the faculty that directs choice of object). T2's somatic location (H2250 wound — body) explains the cross-registry connections to R30 contrition and R151 sorrow at Isa 53:5 — all three characteristics meet at the somatic-constitutional level in that verse.

T2's constitutional levels (soul, heart, desire-faculty, somatic) are each evidenced by specific lexical items that T7 analyses. Soul-level (Ecc 9:4 — H2266 Qal); heart-level (Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23 — H2270, H2266 Hithpael); somatic (H2250 — Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5). The grammatical range (T7.1.2) is the lexical expression of the constitutional range (T2): the Hithpael (volitional self-joining) is the heart-level agentive act; the Pual (being-joined — passive settled state) is the constitutional fixing endpoint; the adjectival terms (H2270/H2278 — companion/consort) are the heart-level dispositional forms. T7 provides the vocabulary basis for T2's constitutional account.

T3's faculty engagements all run in the same relational direction that T4 identifies as primary (vertical — human toward God). Perception, volition, moral evaluation, conscientiousness, and relational capacity are all deepened when directed toward the right fellowship object (God and God-fearers). T3's bilateral finding (right fellowship deepens; wrong fellowship degrades) explains T4's structural negative (misdirected fellowship is more dangerous than absence — Hos 4:17, Mat 23:30): when every faculty is progressively shaped by the fellowship object, misdirected fellowship progressively degrades every faculty, not just the relational capacity.

T3's snapshot (what fellowship does to faculties in the moment) and T5's longitudinal account (what habitual fellowship produces over time) together reveal that faculty deepening (T3) is the process of which constitutional character formation (T5) is the endpoint. The right-direction faculty deepening (T3 — perception, cognition, conscience, moral evaluation each deepened by right fellowship) accumulates over time into the fixed character of Psa 119:63's declared God-fearer companion, which is on its way to Jer 13:23's constitutional formation. T3 → T5 is the moment-to-character arc.

T3's moral evaluation and conscience faculty engagements explain the co-occurrence pattern with desire (R43) in T6. Desire (R43) directs fellowship; conscience assesses the moral quality of the fellowship object; moral evaluation confirms or challenges the direction. The three characteristics (desire, fellowship, conscience) operate in sequence: desire directs → conscience evaluates → fellowship forms. The T6 co-occurrence signal between R43 and R062 is the shared-verse evidence of this sequential relationship that T3's faculty analysis illuminates.

T7's literary forms explain why T3 finds different faculty engagements in different evidential contexts. The wisdom poetry setting (Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23, Ecc 9:4) engages primarily the volitional, conscientiousness, and moral evaluation faculties. The apostolic epistolary setting (Phili 3:10, 2Cor 1:7, 2Pe 1:4, 1Jo 1:3) engages primarily the relational capacity, affect, and agency faculties. The prophetic setting (Hos 4:17, Isa 53:5, Jer 13:23) engages primarily the moral evaluation and conscience faculties (diagnostic-corrective function). Literary form determines which faculty register the fellowship vocabulary is deployed in.

T4's relational architecture (vertical primary; horizontal derived) and T5's formation arc (habituation → constitutional character) are structurally connected through 1Jo 1:3: the horizontal fellowship is derived from and grounded in the vertical (T4), and the vertical fellowship is the source from which the horizontal formation extends (T5 — the person who has received divine fellowship can extend human fellowship that is grounded in that divine reality). T4's scope-narrows-as-depth-increases finding is T5's formation telos: the deepest fellowship (divine nature participation — 2Pe 1:4) is also the narrowest in scope and the furthest eschatological endpoint.

T4's relational directions and T6's structural connections are expressing the same reality from two perspectives. The God-to-human direction (T4.1 — divine initiative) corresponds to the R180 yielding connection (T6 — shared anchor 2Pe 1:4): the divine initiative at 2Pe 1:4 requires an inner human yielding (escape from corruption) as its receiving condition. The human-to-human giving direction (T4.3 — companionship, covenant, comfort-sharing) corresponds to the R34 covenant, R192 comfort, and R43 desire connections in T6. The relational directions (T4) and the structural cross-registry connections (T6) are the inner-being and cross-registry expressions of the same relational movements.

T4's relational directions map directly onto T7's literary forms. The divine-to-human direction (T4.1) is primarily carried by the apostolic epistolary setting (2Pe 1:4, 2Cor 13:14, 1Jo 1:3). The human-to-God direction (T4.2) is primarily carried by wisdom poetry (Psa 119:63, Phili 3:10) and apostolic letter (Phili 3:10). The human-giving direction (T4.3) is carried by wisdom poetry (Ecc 4:10), apostolic letter (2Cor 1:7), and covenantal text (Mal 2:14). The literary form determines which relational direction is expressed: the apostolic letter form enables the most explicit vertical formulation; wisdom poetry enables the most explicit horizontal formulation.

T5's formation mechanisms (participatory transfer and wound-as-cleansing) explain why fellowship is structurally connected in T6 to both desire (R43 — participatory-transfer mechanism begins with desire-directed alignment) and to contrition/sorrow (R30, R151 — wound-as-cleansing mechanism meets contrition and sorrow at Isa 53:5). T5's two formation mechanisms generate two distinct clusters of T6 structural connections: the habitual-alignment cluster (R43 desire, R167 unity, R34 covenant) and the suffering-cleansing cluster (R30 contrition, R151 sorrow, R192 comfort). Formation mechanism determines structural cluster.

T5's formation sequence (volitional alignment → habituation → constitutional fixing) is grounded in T7's verse evidence. The sequence beginning point (volitional alignment) is carried by Psa 119:63 (wisdom poetry — declared dispositional alignment). The habituation arc is carried by the prophetic diagnostic setting (Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17 — naming the fixed endpoint of wrong habitual fellowship). The constitutional telos (divine nature participation) is carried by the apostolic-theological setting (2Pe 1:4). T7's literary forms track the formation sequence from beginning (wisdom poetry) through middle (prophetic diagnosis) to eschatological telos (apostolic theology).

T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis are mutually grounding. The wound-companionship shared root (T6.4 — H2250 wound and H2270 companion both from chavar) is T7's single most significant etymology finding: the same root that produces companionship produces wound, revealing that the deepest fellowship is structurally wound-rooted. T6's primary anchor verses (Isa 53:5, 2Pe 1:4, 1Jo 1:3, Mal 2:14 as cross-registry hubs) are also T7's most analytically rich verses — the verses that carry the most structural connections are the same verses that are most exegetically developed in the primary anchor analysis.

T1 establishes forgiveness as an act; T2 maps where the act is constitutionally located. The four modes (T1) each engage different constitutional levels (T2): covenantal-objective mode engages no named inner-being locus (external mechanism); divine relational-personal mode engages the full constitutional range (spirit, heart, cognition — via the access-postures of faith, repentance, confession); human horizontal mode explicitly requires the heart (Mat 18:35); intercessory mode requires the dependence-posture at the constitutional level of the plea. The somatic compassion origin-point (T2.5 — splagchnizomai) is the constitutional expression of the volitional-release act (T1.4.1 — the act must be chosen). Definition and constitutional location are co-determining: the mode of forgiveness determines which constitutional level is engaged.

T1's four modes explain T3's faculty engagement pattern. The covenantal-objective mode (no inner-being prerequisite) corresponds to T3's absence of any faculty requirement for that mode. The divine relational-personal mode (faith, repentance, confession as access-postures) engages volition, conscience, and cognition. The human horizontal mode ("from your heart") requires volition, conscience, and moral evaluation. The intercessory mode requires the dependence-posture that engages cognition (accurate assessment of the grounds of the plea — God's character, not human merit) and volition. T1's definition of forgiveness as transitional (not terminal) is confirmed by T3's finding that every faculty engaged by forgiveness is deepened toward further engagement — not closed by the act but opened by it.

T1's four modes map directly onto T4's relational directions. Covenantal-objective mode = God-to-human (cultic ritual mediated); divine relational-personal = God-to-human (personal declaratory); intercessory = human-to-God on behalf of others; human horizontal = human-giving and human-receiving. The vertical-horizontal interdependence (T4 — the structural claim that receiving divine forgiveness enables extending human forgiveness) is the relational expression of T1's layered modes (the horizontal mode assumes the divine relational-personal mode has operated). T1's structural negative (retained claim = inner torment) is T4's receiving-blocked condition (Hos 4:17 parallel — wrong prior alignment blocks reception of right offering; Mat 18:35 — unforgiveness calling into question whether divine forgiveness was genuinely received).

T1 establishes forgiveness as transitional (not terminal); T5 confirms this and maps the formation arc. T1's downstream states (love, reverence) are T5's immediate formation products. T1's sustained conditions (freed access to God, enabled worship, capacity to extend) are T5's longer-arc formation outcomes. T1's eschatological orientation (Jer 31:34 as the forward-pointing fullness) is T5's eschatological telos. The two mechanisms in T5 (declaratory and atoning) correspond to T1's two primary mode-types: the divine relational-personal mode operates through the declaratory-atoning mechanism; the human horizontal mode operates through the heart-level-release mechanism. T1 and T5 together reveal the complete arc from definition to formation endpoint.

T1's definitional structure explains T6's co-occurrence pattern with precision. Guilt-073 (51 shared verses) is the definitional counterpart (T1 — forgiveness releases guilt; they are structurally inseparable). Mercy-111 (15 shared) is the generating disposition (T1 — God's mercy is what produces the forgiving act). Repentance-135 (12 shared) is the access-posture (T1 — human side receiving condition). Love-103 (8 shared) is the named downstream state (T1 — Luk 7:47). Faith-059 (9 shared) is the receiving instrument (T1). Every major co-occurrence registry corresponds to a logical role in T1's definitional structure. T6's co-occurrence data is therefore a reading of the complete forgiveness transaction from the outside — each co-registry names one element of the transaction that T1 defines from the inside.

T1's definitional structure is encoded in the vocabulary's lexical and grammatical architecture (T7). T1's three Hebrew form-types (act, disposition, possession) correspond precisely to the three Hebrew terms (H5545 verb, H5546 adjective, H5547 noun). T1's four modes map onto the vocabulary's grammatical range (Niphal passive = covenantal-objective received status; Qal active = divine forgiving act; human commanded = G0863H in imperative/hortatory forms; seeking = H5545 imperative in intercessory prayer). T7's cross-testament development finding (NT applies divine-forgiveness vocabulary to commanded human forgiveness) is the lexical expression of T1's structural claim about the human horizontal mode being shaped by the divine model. T1 and T7 are mutually encoded — the vocabulary is structured to carry the definition.

T2's constitutional locations map onto T3's faculty engagements. The heart-locus (T2.3 — Mat 18:35) corresponds to T3's volition, moral evaluation, and conscience faculties — all heart-level. The cognition-locus (T2.4 — Jer 31:34) corresponds to T3's cognition and memory faculty engagements. The somatic-compassion locus (T2.5 — splagchnizomai) corresponds to T3's affect faculty at its somatic depth. The constitutional movement (T2.10 — guilt-bearing → forgiveness → love) is the sequence in which T3's faculties are engaged: conscience identifies the guilt → volition releases the claim → affect produces the love-response. Constitutional location determines faculty engagement sequence.

T2's constitutional origin (divine character as the source — Psa 86:5, Psa 130:4) explains T4's God-to-human directional dominance. Because forgiveness originates exclusively in divine character at the deepest level, the primary relational direction is necessarily God-initiated toward the human. T2's heart-locus requirement (Mat 18:35) explains T4's giving-direction requirement: genuine horizontal forgiveness requires the same constitutional depth as the received vertical (heart-level in both cases). The somatic-compassion origin (T2.5) is the constitutional enabler of the horizontal-giving direction (T4.3): the person who genuinely gives forgiveness does so from a somatic-affective inner origin (splagchnizomai), not merely a verbal performance.

T2's constitutional movement (guilt-bearing → forgiveness → love → capacity to extend) is T5's formation sequence described at the constitutional level. T2's finding that the constitutional event produces further inner states (love, direct access to God) is T5's transitional-formative account. The body-deposit gap (T2.8 — data silent; T5.7 closed) means the formation is entirely inner-constitutional — the formative event leaves no somatic residue, which is consistent with T5's finding that forgiveness is transitional rather than permanently marking. The eschatological telos (T5.6 — Jer 31:34) is the permanent constitutional state that present forgiveness-events anticipate.

T2's cognitive-locus finding (Jer 31:34 — forgiveness engages and enables cognition) explains the R160 thought co-occurrence at Jer 31:34. T2's somatic-compassion locus (splagchnizomai — Mat 18:27) explains the R023 compassion co-occurrence (4 shared verses; DIM-064-SD002 on splagchnizomai as somatic precondition). T2's heart-locus requirement (Mat 18:35) explains the R073 guilt co-occurrence at the heart level — guilt is the heart-level prior condition that forgiveness addresses at the heart level. Constitutional location and co-occurrence signals are mutually confirming: every major constitutional locus of forgiveness corresponds to a co-occurrence registry that operates in the same constitutional space.

T2's constitutional levels are each evidenced by specific grammatical forms in T7. Heart-locus (Mat 18:35 — heart-level requirement) is carried by the G0863H imperative forms in the horizontal commands. Cognition-locus (Jer 31:34) is carried by the H5545 Niphal passive (the worshipper as receiver) and the se.li.chah noun (forgiveness as the quality God has, which enables the cognitive-relational access). Somatic-compassion locus (Mat 18:27 — splagchnizomai) is outside the registry's own vocabulary but is confirmed as the constitutional origin point of the horizontal forgiving act. T7 provides the grammatical evidence base for T2's constitutional account.

T3's faculty engagements all point in the same relational directions that T4 identifies as primary. Conscience (T3.9) is engaged in both the seeking-forgiveness direction (human-to-God: confession as conscience's public verdict) and the giving direction (human horizontal: the conscience that recognises the offence to be released). Memory (T3.3) is engaged in the God-to-human direction (divine "remember no more" — Jer 31:34) and the human-giving direction (releasing the memory's active use against the offender). Volition (T3.6) drives the giving direction (heart-level release is chosen). The vertical-horizontal interdependence (T4 structural claim) is the relational expression of T3's conscience-and-volition faculty account: the person whose conscience has accurately received the magnitude of divine forgiveness has the volitional capacity to extend horizontal forgiveness.

T3's faculty deepenings (cognition enabled — Jer 31:34; love produced — Luk 7:47; conscience purified — Heb 9:14; volition redirected) are T5's formation products described at the faculty level. The formation sequence (T5) is the longitudinal account of which T3's faculty engagements are the synchronic snapshots. The commanded forgiveness as sanctification discipline (T5.5 — Eph 4:32) is the formation vehicle through which the volitional faculty (T3.6) is habituated and the moral evaluation faculty (T3.8) is deepened. T3 and T5 together reveal the full formation arc: T3 shows what happens to each faculty in the moment of forgiveness; T5 shows what sustained habitual forgiveness does to the person over time.

T3's memory faculty finding (Jer 31:34 — divine "remember no more") explains the R160 thought/knowledge co-occurrence at that verse: the divine cognitive act of non-remembering and the human cognitive outcome of knowing God meet in the same verse because they are structurally connected — the guilt-barrier's removal (forgiveness) enables the cognitive-relational access (knowing God). T3's affect-faculty finding (love produced — Luk 7:47) explains the R103 love co-occurrence (8 shared verses) — love is the affective downstream product of forgiveness. T3's conscience faculty finding confirms the R073 guilt structural partnership — guilt is what conscience identifies as the prior condition that forgiveness addresses. Faculty engagements (T3) and structural co-occurrences (T6) are mutually explanatory.

T7's literary forms explain why T3 finds different faculty engagements in different evidential contexts. The cultic-legal corpus (Lev 4:20) — covenantal-objective mode — requires no named faculty engagement (T3 finds no faculty requirement for this mode). The prophetic corpus (Jer 31:34) primarily engages cognition and memory (T3.2, T3.3). The Synoptic narrative corpus (Luk 7:47, Mat 18:27, Mat 18:35) engages affect, volition, and conscience most richly. The apostolic letter corpus (Eph 1:7, Eph 4:32) grounds the formation dimension (T3.10 conscientiousness, T5.5 sanctification). Each literary form carries specific faculty registers — T7's literary analysis explains T3's faculty engagement distribution.

T4's vertical-horizontal interdependence (the structural claim that receiving divine forgiveness enables extending human forgiveness) is T5's formation arc described at the relational level. The formation sequence (T5 — forgiveness received → love generated → capacity to extend formed) is the inner development through which T4's structural interdependence is created: the person who genuinely receives divine forgiveness and generates the love-response (T5) is the person who has the genuine volitional capacity to extend horizontal forgiveness (T4). The eschatological telos (T5.6 — Jer 31:34) is also T4's permanent relational state: the guilt-barrier permanently removed produces permanently restored relational access to God.

T4's relational directions correspond to T6's structural connections. The God-to-human direction (T4.1) corresponds to the mercy-111 connection (mercy as the divine disposition generating the act, operating in the same direction). The human-to-God direction (T4.2) corresponds to the repentance-135 and faith-059 connections (the access-postures that operate in the seeking direction). The horizontal giving-direction (T4.3) corresponds to the love-103 connection (love as the upstream motivator of horizontal forgiveness — Eph 4:32) and the reconciliation-130 connection (Mat 5:24 — reconciliation as the restored state that horizontal forgiveness produces). T4's relational architecture and T6's co-occurrence network are different analytical lenses on the same structural reality.

T4's relational directions map onto T7's literary forms. The God-to-human direction (T4.1 — dominant) is carried by the cultic-legal corpus (H5545 Niphal — God forgives, people receive), the prophetic corpus (Jer 31:34 — divine forgiveness promise), and the apostolic letter corpus (Eph 1:7 — through his blood). The human-to-God direction (T4.2 — seeking) is carried by the penitential psalm corpus (Psa 130, Dan 9) and the Synoptic narrative (1Jo 1:9). The horizontal-giving direction (T4.3) is carried by the Synoptic parable corpus (Mat 18:23-35) and the apostolic command corpus (Eph 4:32; Luk 17:3-4). T7's literary forms are the evidential vehicles for T4's relational directions — each direction has a primary literary home.

T5's formation sequence (forgiveness received → love → capacity to extend) explains why T6 finds love-103 as a co-occurrence signal (8 shared verses) — love is the formation product most visibly evidenced in the co-occurrence data. T5's two mechanisms (declaratory and atoning) explain the guilt-073 co-occurrence dominance (51 shared verses) — guilt is the condition both mechanisms address, and so it co-occurs across the entire forgiveness corpus. T5's sanctification-formation dimension (T5.5 — Eph 4:32) explains the reconciliation-130 shared anchor (Mat 5:24): the commanded forgiveness in the formation context is structurally connected to the restored relational state (reconciliation) as the goal of the horizontal act. T5's formation mechanisms generate T6's structural connections.

T5's formation sequence is grounded in T7's verse evidence. The access-posture phase (T5.2 — repentance, faith, confession) is carried by the Synoptic and apostolic letter corpus (T7.2 — Act 2:38, Act 10:43, 1Jo 1:9). The forgiveness-received status change is carried by the Synoptic pronouncement corpus (Luk 7:47-48, Mar 2:5). The eschatological telos (T5.6 — Jer 31:34) is the prophetic-eschatological corpus (T7.2). T7's cross-testament vocabulary finding (NT applying divine-forgiveness vocabulary to human forgiveness — T7.1.9 and Q&A-179) is T5's formation mechanism expressed at the lexical level: the commanded human forgiveness ("as God in Christ forgave you" — Eph 4:32) is the formation vehicle, and the vocabulary is the structural carrier of the "as" claim.

T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis confirm and explain each other. The salach root's lexical isolation (T6.4 — no root-sharing with other programme registries; T7.1.1 — no XREF terms) reveals that forgiveness maintains a lexically distinct divine-exclusive domain — the strongest co-occurrence registry (guilt-073 at 51 shared verses) operates in the same semantic territory but at the verse level, not at the root-sharing level. This means the structural partnership between forgiveness and guilt is relational-definitional, not lexical-etymological. The Jer 31:34 multi-registry convergence (T6.6 — shared with guilt, love, thought) is also T7's most analytically rich verse (T7.2.5 — the primary anchor trio includes Jer 31:34): the verse that carries the most structural connections is the verse that is exegetically richest, confirmed from both directions.

T1's four-kind structure (disposition, standing, capacity, character quality) maps onto T2's constitutional levels. The divine-disposition kind operates at the level of grace's origin (T2.9 — God's own disposition; not a human constitutional level). The standing kind operates at the soul level (T2.2 — Rom 5:2 "this grace in which we stand"). The capacity kind operates at the spirit-soul interface — the specific enabling for specific tasks (2Cor 12:9) is received at the deepest level and expressed through specific faculties. The character-quality kind operates at the soul-to-body level (T2.7 — gracious conduct and speech expressing the received inner character outward). T1's constitutional movement finding (SD021 four-faces sequence) is the longitudinal form of T2's spatial map: T2 identifies the constitutional levels; T1 identifies the movement between them.

T1's immediate response pattern (fear → mourning → prostration as the creaturely recognition response) is the affective-volitional expression of T3's faculty engagement at the moment grace arrives. T3.4 (affect is the first named faculty engaged by grace) and T3.6 (the agency-constituting paradox of 1Cor 15:10) together explain why T1.5 finds the creaturely recognition response before positive states settle: grace first produces the affective recognition (fear, mourning) before constituting the agency through which the person responds (prostration as the body enacting the inner posture that grace has produced). T1's finding that grace requires creaturely recognition to complete its operation (T1.7) is T3.1's finding at the faculty level: grace requires and enables perception simultaneously.

T1's four modes of operation map onto T4's four relational directions. The sovereign-bestowal mode corresponds to the God-to-human direction (T4.1 — primary and dominant). The covenantal-relational-sustaining mode corresponds to the relational scope (T4.5 — grace operating within and across covenantal boundaries). The capacity-conferring mode corresponds to the human-to-God direction in its supplication register (T4.2 — SD026: grace giving even the capacity to appeal). The character-forming mode corresponds to the human-giving direction (T4.3 — gracious conduct and speech as the horizontal expression of received grace-character). The vocabulary's structural insight (T4 synthesis: giving-side and receiving-side built from the same root, chen/tachanum) explains T1's finding that grace requires the receptor's inner engagement to complete its operation — the vocabulary encodes the two-sidedness because grace is constitutively a two-sided circuit.

T1's sustained-effect finding (immediate/sustained contrast as qualitative, not merely temporal — what grace does immediately is creaturely recognition; what it does over time is constitution of identity and character) maps onto T5's formation mechanisms. The encounter mechanism (T5) produces the immediate effects named in T1.5; the word, inference, and mediated-transformation mechanisms (T5) produce the sustained effects of T1.6. T5's five mechanisms collectively explain how the four kinds named in T1.2 are produced across different temporal and contextual registers: disposition-receipt is the encounter mechanism; standing is the spirit-outpouring mechanism; capacity is the mediated-transformation mechanism; character quality is the sustained word-and-inference mechanism. T1 names what is produced; T5 names how each kind is produced.

T1's dimension classification (Relational Disposition as primary) and T6's co-occurrence pattern are mutually confirming. The seeking co-occurrence dominance (R140 — 40 verses) is the co-occurrence expression of T1's finding that grace requires the receptor's inner posture of appeal: seeking is the relational posture before grace arrives, and the vocabulary encodes this (tachanum — built into the same root as chen). T6's structural uniqueness finding (grace is not produced by any other characteristic) is T1's definitional consequence: because grace is constitutively the uncaused inclination of the greater toward the lesser, it cannot be produced by anything in the lesser's constitutional system. T1's definition predicts T6's structural uniqueness.

T1's four-kind structure is encoded in T7's vocabulary with structural completeness. The disposition kind (chen, charis as noun) has dedicated terms. The act kind (chanan, charizō) has dedicated verbal forms. The completed-state kind (charitoō — NT coinage, G5487) required a new word because existing vocabulary could not carry the concept of standing-in-favour as a completed, continuing state. The character-quality kind (chen in conduct and speech — 889-003) is carried by chen's adjectival and behavioural register. The vocabulary is structurally complete for the four kinds — each kind has a corresponding lexical form. T7's NT coinage finding (charitoō) is the lexical evidence that T1's four-kind analysis is correct: the fourth kind (standing as completed state) required new vocabulary because it was conceptually unique.

T2's constitutional movement (spirit reception → soul standing → capacity → character expression) maps onto T3's faculty engagement sequence. The spirit-reception stage (T2.1) corresponds to T3.1 (perception — grace enabling the inner sight and hearing that receives it). The soul-standing stage (T2.2) corresponds to T3.9 (conscience — grace as the condition within which conscience can operate without destruction) and T3.8 (moral evaluation — the standing produces the clarity of moral self-assessment). The capacity-gifting stage (T2.1 — spirit level) corresponds to T3.7 (agency — capacity given constitutes agency for the task). The character-expression stage (T2.7 — outward through body-soul) corresponds to T3.10 (conscientiousness — the integrated moral-action expression of received grace-character). T2 maps the constitutional space; T3 maps the faculties active at each spatial level; the two tiers describe the same movement from orthogonal perspectives.

T2's origin analysis (grace does not originate in the human constitutional system — it arrives from God) and T4's directional analysis (God-to-human is primary and dominant) are the same finding from constitutional and relational perspectives respectively. The spirit level as reception point (T2.1) and the God-to-human direction as primary (T4.1) are mutually explanatory: because grace originates with God and is received at the spirit level, the primary relational direction is necessarily God-initiated toward the human spirit. The human-to-God direction (T4.2 — supplication) corresponds to T2.10's constitutional movement in reverse: the soul's recognition of need translates into the somatic enactment of supplication (prostration, fasting, sackcloth), which is T2.7's bidirectional body-soul finding. Constitutional location and relational direction are co-determining.

T2's constitutional movement (SD021 sequence: disposition → standing → capacity → character) and T5's five formation mechanisms describe the same formation arc from spatial and temporal perspectives. T2 identifies the constitutional levels through which grace moves; T5 identifies the mechanisms by which grace moves through each level in different contexts. The encounter mechanism (T5 — sudden specific relational event) produces the spirit-level reception (T2.1 — the initial point of arrival). The word mechanism (T5 — Act 20:32 — grace through proclaimed word) produces the soul-standing establishment (T2.2 — the word's sustained effect on the person's constitutive condition). The mediated-transformation mechanism (T5 — tenderheartedness → forgiving — Eph 4:32, SD030) corresponds to T2.7's bidirectional body-soul finding: the inner transformation mediates outward through the body-soul interface. T2.8's open question (body deposit) and T5.7's open status are formally aligned — both pending further investigation.

T2's constitutional origin finding (grace does not originate in the human constitutional system) and T6's structural uniqueness finding (grace is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic) are the same finding from constitutional and structural perspectives. They confirm each other from different analytical directions: T2 establishes that there is no constitutional source for grace within the human person; T6 establishes that there is no characteristic-level source for grace within the programme's inner-being landscape. The vocabulary's structural completeness (T7 — but relevant here as T6.4) reflects this constitutional uniqueness: because grace originates entirely from outside the human system, its vocabulary must encode the full two-sided circuit (giving and receiving) from within a single root, with no gap left for a generative human-side term.

T2's constitutional levels are carried by specific grammatical forms in T7. The spirit-reception level (T2.1) is carried by the perfect passive form of charitoō (kecharitōmenē — the completed act of being-made-the-object-of-grace, with continuing state) — the most precise grammatical encoding of a constitutional arrival that has permanently changed the person's standing. The soul-standing level (T2.2) is carried by charis as noun (the inhabited state of favour) and chen as the relational standing-in-favour term. The character-expression level is carried by chen in its conduct/speech register (889-003) and charizō in its human-forgiving mode. T7's NT coinage finding (charitoō invented to carry the standing-in-favour concept) is the lexical evidence that T2's spirit-level reception was conceptually unprecedented — no prior vocabulary could name what happened constitutionally when grace-as-standing arrived.

T3's agency-constituting finding (grace constitutes rather than replaces agency — 1Cor 15:10) and T4's human-to-God direction (supplication as the human posture of appeal — requiring genuine human agency in the act of plea) are structurally aligned. The constituted agency (T3.7) is precisely the agency that supplication requires: the creature must genuinely appeal, not merely receive passively. T3's finding that grace and conscience are mutually necessary (T3.9) corresponds to T4's relational-access finding: grace transforms the threshold of human-to-God approach (prosagōgē — SD035) from impassable to open, which is the relational expression of T3.9's constitutional finding that grace creates the condition within which conscience can operate fully without destruction. T3 names the inner-being event; T4 names the relational consequence of the same event.

T3's faculty findings are the synchronic expression of T5's diachronic formation arc. The creaturely recognition response (T5.2 — fear → mourning → prostration as the consistent transitional state in all grace-sequences) is the affective-faculty event (T3.4) at the moment of grace-arrival, which then constitutes the volitional-faculty alignment (T3.6 — the agency-constituting paradox) that enables the conscientious expression (T3.10). T5's formation arc (sustained work producing character-quality) is the longitudinal development of T3.9's finding (conscience operating fully within grace) into T3.10's finding (conscientiousness as the integrated moral character that grace constitutes over time). The person who has been formed by grace through the T5 formation arc is the person in whom T3's faculty engagements have been progressively deepened and integrated.

T3's faculty findings explain several patterns in T6's co-occurrence landscape. The seeking co-occurrence dominance (R140 — 40 verses) is partly explained by T3.6's agency-constituting finding: seeking requires the constituted agency that grace provides for its own reception (SD026 — grace giving the capacity for supplication). The power cluster co-occurrences (strength R187 — 15 verses, might R198 — 10 verses) confirm T3.7's agency-constituting finding at the cross-registry level: grace producing agency is registered as co-occurring with power/strength across the corpus. T3.8's moral clarity finding explains the guilt co-occurrence (R073 — 10 verses): the moral clarity grace produces is most visible at the point of moral failure, and the co-occurrence reflects this — grace and guilt appear together because grace produces the moral clarity that accurately names the guilt it addresses.

T3's faculty findings are grounded in and confirmed by T7's primary anchor evidence. The perceptive-faculty finding (T3.1 — grace requires and enables perception) is grounded in the eye-formula of chen (SD041), Zec 12:10's grace-produced vision, and SD012's hearing-receptivity pattern. T7's argument structure for Zec 12:10 (grace outpoured → "when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced") confirms T3.1 precisely: the grace-event produces and requires the inner sight that recognises what grace has cost. T3.6's agency-constituting paradox (1Cor 15:10) is T7.3's most significant framework-divergence point: at the moment of constitutional incapacity, all human science frameworks assume the person has some prior agency; the biblical account holds the paradox of grace-constituted agency that is genuinely human while grace-originated. This is the methodological divergence T7.3 identifies and T3.6 establishes as the operative finding.

T4's relational directions correspond to T5's formation mechanisms. The God-to-human direction (T4.1 — sovereign bestowal) corresponds to the encounter and spirit-outpouring mechanisms (T5.3 — sudden specific relational event; Zec 12:10). The human-to-God direction (T4.2 — supplication) corresponds to the sustained-formation arc of T5.5: the person who habitually supplicates is being formed into the character that supplicates, which is itself the character-quality kind identified in T1. The human-giving direction (T4.3 — gracious forgiving, speech-grace) corresponds to T5.3's mediated-transformation mechanism (grace → tenderheartedness → forgiving → gracious speech — SD030, Eph 4:32). T4 describes the relational shape of each mechanism; T5 describes the inner formation process each mechanism produces.

T4's relational architecture and T6's co-occurrence network express the same structural reality from relational and cross-registry perspectives. The seeking co-occurrence dominance (T6 — R140, 40 verses) is the co-occurrence expression of T4.2 (the human-to-God supplication direction): seeking is the relational posture, and R140 is the registry that names it. The sequential relationship confirmed in T6.2 (grace precedes and enables faith, repentance, supplication, mourning — SD003, SD006, SD026, SD038) is the causal expression of T4's architectural finding: grace is the enabling condition for every positive human-to-God relational posture. The boundary questions in T6.5 (grace-love, grace-mercy, grace-chesed) correspond to T4.5's scope analysis: the characteristics that share grace's scope (particularly love and mercy) are the same characteristics whose relational scope overlaps with grace's at T4.5.

T4's most analytically distinctive finding (the vocabulary encodes the two-sided grace-circuit in a single root: chen/chanan for giving; tachanum/techinah for seeking) is T7's most significant root-level finding. T7.1 confirms: the chen root family embeds both the giving side (chen — favour, chanan — to be gracious) and the receiving-seeking side (tachanum, techinah — supplication) in a single etymological family. This is unique in the programme's vocabulary data: no other characteristic has the giving-side and receiving-side vocabulary built from the same root. T4's relational architecture and T7's lexical analysis are the relational and lexical descriptions of the same structural reality — that grace is constitutively a two-sided circuit whose two sides are inseparable at the root level.

T5's five formation mechanisms explain the structural pattern visible in T6's co-occurrence and sequential landscape. The encounter mechanism (sudden grace-event) corresponds to the sequences confirmed in T6.2 (grace precedes and causes the immediate responses of mourning, fear, supplication — SD038, SD026). The word mechanism (Act 20:32) corresponds to the faith co-occurrence (R059 — 10 verses): the proclaimed word that carries grace produces faith as its response. The inference mechanism (Rom 8:32 — assurance derived by reasoning) corresponds to the hope and peace co-occurrences (R117 — 10 verses): reasoned assurance from grace produces the inner peace and hope co-occurring registries name. The mediated-transformation mechanism (tenderheartedness → forgiving) produces the love co-occurrence (R103 — 14 verses) as tenderheartedness is the inner precondition of the forgiving that love and grace share. T5's mechanisms generate T6's structural connections.

T5's most distinctive formation finding (five mechanisms, creaturely recognition response as consistent transitional state, grace inhabiting rather than resolving suffering) is grounded in and confirmed by T7's vocabulary and textual evidence. The NT coinage charitoō (T7.1 P9 — coined for standing-in-favour as completed, continuing state) is the lexical ground of T5.1's irreversibility finding: the perfect passive grammatical form encodes precisely the "transformation not reversed" character that T5.1 identifies. T7.2's argument structure analysis (Zec 12:10 — grace outpoured → recognition → mourning → supplication) is the textual grounding for T5.2's creaturely recognition response finding: the sequence is not an incidental narrative feature but an argued structural pattern. T7.3's framework divergence (human science frameworks assuming prior capacity; Scripture denying it at the point of maximum incapacity — Eph 2:1-5) is the methodological expression of T5.5's deepest formation finding: the person cannot initiate their own formation by grace. T5 and T7 describe the same formation reality from narrative and lexical angles.

T6's structural uniqueness (grace is not produced by any inner-being characteristic — categorically the originating point of the causal chain) and T7's vocabulary structural completeness (every dimension of grace's operation has a corresponding term, including the structural negative acharistos) confirm and explain each other. T6 establishes that grace stands outside the characteristic-production network; T7 establishes that the vocabulary encodes this structural position with unique completeness — including the negative (acharistos) and the NT coinage (charitoō) as markers of grace's conceptual uniqueness. The shared anchor verses (T6.6) are also T7's most evidentially rich texts: Eph 2:8 (which anchors multiple co-occurring registries) carries the most complete single-verse expression of grace's structural position (sovereign gift, not of works, producing faith). T6 and T7 mutually confirm that grace's structural uniqueness in the programme landscape corresponds to a lexical uniqueness in the vocabulary — both point to the same underlying analytical claim: grace is categorically distinct from every other inner-being characteristic.

What kindness is (T1) and where it resides constitutionally (T2) are tightly integrated. T1's working definition — stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill, constituted by faithfulness/compassion/forgiveness — is realised at the heart level (T2). The heart is not merely the storage location of kindness but the site where its constituent elements are inscribed as character (Pro 3:3 — "write them on the tablet of your heart"). The T2 constitutional movement pattern (Spirit-formation → heart-inscription → relational disposition → deliberate acts) maps directly onto T1's three modes (Spirit formation / deliberate act / dispositional orientation) — the movement pattern is the mechanism by which the T1 disposition comes into being. The T1 finding that kindness is composite (faithfulness + justice + compassion + forgiveness) is structurally consistent with T2's finding that it is a heart-level characteristic: the heart in Scripture is precisely the site where complex moral orientation is integrated — not a single emotion but the whole-person covenantal direction.

T1's finding that kindness is primarily a disposition (not merely an act) is confirmed and deepened by T3's faculty analysis. The disposition identified in T1 requires the full engagement of multiple faculties — volition (it is chosen), agency (it is enacted), conscientiousness (moral awareness + will + action integrated), and relational capacity (it constitutes relationship quality) — which together explain why it is dispositional rather than episodic: a characteristic that engages this many faculties simultaneously is, by its nature, a settled whole-person orientation rather than a single-faculty response. T1's three-mode finding (disposition / deliberate act / Spirit formation) is reflected in T3's faculty pattern: the dispositional mode engages conscientiousness and relational capacity; the deliberate act mode engages volition and agency; the Spirit-formation mode produces the integrated faculty engagement over time. The counterfeit (chrēstologia — T1.4.3 / Q&A-024) maps onto T3's faculty analysis as the pathology of the speech-mode: it engages the communicative faculty while bypassing genuine moral evaluation and conscientiousness — presence of outward form without inner faculty integration.

T1's definition of kindness as other-oriented loyal goodwill is perfectly reflected in T4's relational interface structure: kindness has no meaningful existence except in the relational directions T4 maps. The T4 finding that divine-to-human is the most extensively evidenced direction grounds T1's working definition — kindness in its primary form is God's stable outward-oriented goodwill toward the creature, and the human analogue is derived from and dependent on this. The T4 circuit (divine → human → reproduced in giving toward others → communal) maps onto T1's conditions of reception (Q&A-031): the person who has received divine kindness and responded with trust and covenant orientation is precisely the person positioned to extend it to others. T1's finding that kindness explicitly excludes self-serving motivation (Q&A-020) is structurally necessary for T4's circuit to function: if kindness at any point in the circuit becomes self-serving (chrēstologia), the transmission is severed — which is why Eph 4:32 grounds human giving not in the recipient's desert but in the received divine kindness.

T1's finding that kindness is a disposition (not primarily an act) is what makes T5's formative dimension possible. A characteristic that is dispositional can be formed over time — it can be produced by the Spirit, inscribed on the heart, and developed through encounter and practice into the settled chasid identity. If kindness were only an act, T5 would have nothing to form. The T5 finding that three formation mechanisms are present (Spirit-formation, inscription, encounter) maps onto T1's three modes of operation (Spirit-produced / deliberate act / dispositional orientation) — they describe the same structure from different angles. The T1 finding that kindness is composite (faithfulness + compassion + justice + forgiveness) introduces a formative complexity: one does not form kindness monolithically, but rather the constituent elements are co-formed over time, which explains why the chasid's formation is described as a lifetime arc rather than a moment's decision. T1's sustained-effect finding (Q&A-028/029 — transformation from receptive moment to dispositional identity) is the T5 telos stated from the T1 side: the same reality described as T1's sustained effect is T5's formative outcome.

T1's finding that kindness is composite — constituted by faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness — is confirmed and extended by T6's co-occurrence data. The T6 finding that kindness consistently co-occurs with faithfulness (Reg 60), justice (Reg 98), love (Reg 103), and compassion (Reg 23) is not merely a co-occurrence pattern but reflects the T1 structural composition: these co-occurring characteristics are partially internal to kindness itself, not merely adjacent to it. This has a significant implication: kindness cannot be fully studied within a single registry — it requires the cluster analysis that Session D will provide. The T6 causal network (divine kindness → praise + repentance → reproduced human kindness) is the T1 relational mechanism made explicit at the T6 structural level. The T1 boundary finding (kindness is not love, not compassion, not grace — Q&A-021) is where T6's distinction analysis (Q&A-082) begins and cannot resolve without Session D cross-registry work. The two tiers together reveal the tension: kindness is internally composite in ways that make its boundary with adjacent characteristics difficult to draw precisely.

T1's working definition of kindness and T7's lexical foundation finding are in direct productive tension. T7 reveals that the Hebrew chesed cannot be translated as "kindness" without loss of its faithfulness/loyalty dimension — every English rendering is reductive. T1's working definition ("stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill") attempts to bridge this gap by combining both dimensions, but T7's finding that chesed's semantic range spans goodness/kindness/faithfulness as a single unified concept means that the programme's registry name ("kindness") is itself a definitional understatement. This tension is analytically productive: it means that Session C's prose on "what kindness is" must grapple with a characteristic whose Hebrew name is richer than its English label, and that every claim made about kindness in the programme should be held against the chesed evidence where the fuller concept is preserved. T7's finding about chrēstologia as a possible NT coinage also illuminates T1: if chrēstologia was coined precisely to name the pathology of pleasant speech without inner disposition, then the Greek-speaking community around Paul had a clear concept of kindness-as-disposition whose counterfeit needed a specific name — confirming T1's finding that the distinction between genuine and counterfeit kindness was analytically important to the early church.

T2's finding that kindness is located in the heart and moves outward through a constitutional trajectory (Spirit → heart → disposition → acts) connects structurally with T3's finding that kindness engages the heart's integrating faculties — volition, agency, conscientiousness, moral evaluation, relational capacity, and conscience. The heart in T2 is not merely a location label but the integrating site where all these faculties converge (T2.3.2 — Q&A-045). The constitutional movement pattern in T2 (downward-outward from Spirit to acts) is powered by the multi-faculty engagement that T3 documents: the Spirit-formation produces the heart-level disposition, but the heart exercises volition (choosing to be kind), agency (enacting kindness), and conscientiousness (sustaining kindness reliably over time) to move the disposition into action. The T3 finding that kindness engages taste as the primary perceptive faculty (1Pet 2:3 — "tasted that the Lord is good") is the perceptive entry point for the T2 constitutional movement: the person first tastes divine kindness, then the Spirit forms it in the heart, then the heart's faculties exercise it outward. The two tiers together describe a complete inner-to-outer movement with both location (T2) and mechanism (T3) identified.

T2's constitutional location findings (heart as primary site; Spirit as origin) directly enable and ground T4's relational interface findings. The T4 circuit (divine → human → giving → communal) is not possible without a constitutional base that can receive, hold, and transmit kindness — which is exactly what T2's heart-level location provides. The T2 finding that kindness originates in God's own character (Exo 34:6), is Spirit-mediated into the believer (Gal 5:22), and is inscribed on the heart (Pro 3:3) describes the constitutional preparation of the relational equipment T4 maps. The T2 finding that kindness moves outward (soul → body expression) is the constitutional basis for T4's "giving" direction: the inner character disposition (T2) is what makes outward relational giving (T4) possible. Conversely, T4's relational circuit — and specifically the giving direction grounded in received divine kindness (Eph 4:32) — explains why the T2 constitutional movement runs in the direction it does: the constitutional movement is outward because the characteristic is constitutively relational.

T2 and T5 together reveal the complete formation story for kindness. T2 identifies the target — heart-level character formation as the constitutional site where kindness is deposited and inscribed. T5 identifies the mechanisms — Spirit-formation, inscription, and encounter — and the trajectory — from initial reception through formation to chasid identity and eschatological preservation. The T2 finding that no body-deposit exists (T2.8) is structurally consistent with T5's finding that the three formation mechanisms are non-biological: Spirit-formation, inscription, and encounter are all constitutional (spirit/heart level) or relational (encounter), not somatic. The T2 origin finding (triple origin: divine character → Spirit-mediated → humanly formed) maps onto T5's mechanism finding (encounter → inscription → organic Spirit-production) as the same formation process described from different angles — T2 asks where it comes from; T5 asks how it gets there. The eschatological dimension in T5 (the chasid is preserved beyond death) connects with the constitutional permanence finding of T2: a Spirit-formed heart-inscribed character does not dissolve with the body because it was never primarily a body-level deposit.

T2's heart-location finding and T6's structural relationship findings together reveal something about kindness that neither tier alone states: the heart is not only the constitutional site of kindness but the site where kindness's constituent adjacent characteristics (faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness) are also inscribed. Pro 3:3 — "write them on the tablet of your heart" — names the heart as the inscription site for chesed and emet (faithfulness/truth) together, suggesting that the T6 co-occurrence pattern (kindness + faithfulness as the most persistent pair) has a constitutional basis: they are co-inscribed. This means T6's network of adjacent characteristics is not merely relational or contextual co-occurrence but a constitutional clustering: they share the same constitutional site. The T2 constitutional movement outward (heart → acts) and T6's causal network (kindness → reproduced kindness → sustained covenant relationships) trace the same movement from two angles — T2 names the direction, T6 names the mechanism of propagation.

T2's constitutional location findings (heart as primary site; no body deposit; Spirit as origin) are grounded in and confirmed by T7's lexical evidence. The explicit heart-inscription language of Pro 3:3 — the most important T2 finding — is a direct chesed text: the term being inscribed is chesed itself. T7's finding about chesed's semantic range (goodness/kindness/faithfulness as a unified concept) reinforces T2's origin finding: the triple origin structure (divine → Spirit → formed) is exactly what the chesed vocabulary expects — it names God's own inner attribute (Exo 34:6 — abounding in steadfast love), which the Spirit reproduces in the believer, which is then formed through practice and encounter. The T7 LXX finding (chesed → eleos/mercy) has a constitutional implication: the LXX translation emphasises the compassion/affective dimension of chesed over its faithfulness/constitutional dimension — which may explain why the NT Greek kindness vocabulary (chrēstos/chrēstotēs) emphasises quality of character rather than covenant obligation. The two tiers together: lexical depth (T7) explains why constitutional location (T2) lands at the heart — chesed is precisely a heart-level characteristic because it names the covenantal faithfulness that is inscribed in the inner person.

T3's faculty analysis and T4's relational interface analysis are structurally interdependent in a specific way: the faculties that kindness most strongly engages (volition, agency, relational capacity, conscientiousness) are precisely the faculties required for the T4 relational circuit to operate. Relational capacity (T3.11) is what makes the T4 interfaces possible at all — the person must have the constitutional equipment for genuine connection before kindness can flow in any direction. Agency (T3.7) is what makes the T4 giving direction concrete — not passive benevolence but enacted care. Conscientiousness (T3.10) is what makes the T4 circuit sustainable across time — the chasid who reliably keeps covenant. The T4 communal interface (Moses's intercession; the chasidim assembly) requires an intercessory capacity (T3 — memory + relational capacity) that knows how to appeal on behalf of others. The two tiers together describe a portrait of the whole inner person oriented outward: T3 names the inner equipment and T4 names the relational directions in which that equipment is deployed.

T3's faculty findings and T5's formation findings reveal a bidirectional relationship: the faculties that kindness engages are both the tools through which formation occurs and the products of formation. Volition (T3) is exercised in the deliberate acts of kindness (Rut 3:10, 2Sa 10:2) that form the chasid's character — formation happens partly through the repeated exercise of volitional kindness. Conscientiousness (T3) is both engaged by kindness and deepened through its practice — "let not steadfast love forsake you" (Pro 3:3) implies that conscientiousness requires maintenance, and that maintenance in turn deepens conscientiousness. The T5 finding that suffering does not impair kindness formation (Lam 3:22, Job 6:4, Gen 44:29/32) maps onto the T3 faculty engagement in suffering contexts: conscience (T3.9 — the burden of Judah's surety-pledge), moral evaluation (T3.8 — Hos 6:6's priority ranking), and relational capacity (T3.11 — Moses's intercessory solidarity) are all engaged precisely in the suffering mode of kindness. The two tiers together: the inner faculties are both the instruments and the beneficiaries of kindness formation.

T3's finding that kindness engages volition, agency, and conscientiousness as an integrated whole maps onto T6's causal network in a specific way: it is precisely these three faculties operating together that make the T6 causal chain function. The T6 finding that received human kindness causes its reproduction (2Sa 10:2 — "as his father dealt loyally with me, so I will deal loyally") requires all three: the cognition (knowing what was done), the moral evaluation (recognising the obligation), and the agency (acting on it). The T6 co-occurrence finding that kindness clusters with faithfulness, patience, and humility maps onto T3's multi-faculty profile: faithfulness requires conscientiousness; patience requires impulse-control at the volitional level; humility requires conscience and moral evaluation.
